target.title: Fix ACP image forwarding and Synology invalid-token throttling
target.body:
## Summary

This PR bundles two small, low-risk fixes:

1. Fix ACP attachment collection so URL-only image attachments are forwarded into ACP turns.
2. Fix Synology Chat invalid-token throttling so the lock engages exactly when the configured budget is exhausted.

## What changed

### ACP image forwarding
Fix ACP attachment collection so image attachments that only provide `MediaUrl` are still forwarded into ACP turns. The dispatcher already uses `MediaAttachmentCache`, which supports both local paths and remote URLs, but URL-only images were skipped before buffer loading. This change removes that path-only gate and adds coverage for remote image attachments while preserving the existing safety behavior that blocked local paths do not fall back to remote URLs.

### Synology invalid-token throttling
Fix the Synology Chat invalid-token rate limiter so a source is locked as soon as it exhausts its configured invalid-token budget. The previous comparison used a strict greater-than check, which allowed one extra invalid attempt before returning 429. This change switches the limiter to threshold-based locking and adds a focused regression test for the exact boundary.

## Validation

- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts extensions/synology-chat/src/webhook-handler.test.ts`

Result on 2026-05-12 after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`:

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)  # auto-reply shard
Tests       41 passed (41)

Test Files  1 passed (1)  # extension-messaging shard
Tests       23 passed (23)
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** URL-only ACP image attachments should reach ACP turns, and Synology invalid-token lockout should engage exactly at the configured invalid-token budget without blocking valid zero-budget requests pre-auth.

**Real environment tested:** Local Linux checkout of OpenClaw on branch head `ff7aee2b89`, Node/tsx runtime from the repository dependency set, after rebasing onto `origin/main` `5abb4b18c8`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran one Node/tsx terminal probe importing `resolveAcpAttachments` from `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-attachments.ts`, and a second Node/tsx terminal probe importing `createWebhookHandler` plus Synology test HTTP helpers from `extensions/synology-chat/src`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the ACP attachment probe:

```json
{
  "cacheCalls": [
    {
      "url": "https://example.com/inbound.png",
      "maxBytes": 10485760,
      "timeoutMs": 1000
    }
  ],
  "attachments": [
    {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cmVtb3RlLWltYWdl"
    }
  ]
}
```

Terminal output from the Synology webhook probe:

```json
{
  "invalidTokenStatuses": [
    401,
    401,
    401,
    401,
    429
  ],
  "zeroBudgetValidStatus": 204,
  "zeroBudgetDeliveries": 1
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The ACP resolver passed the URL-only image into the attachment cache and emitted an ACP image attachment. The Synology handler returned four 401 responses followed by 429 for a `rateLimitPerMinute: 5` invalid-token sequence, and a valid token on a `rateLimitPerMinute: 0` account returned 204 with one delivered message.

**What was not tested:** No live NAS instance or end-to-end ACP server was used; this proof exercises the exported runtime functions and webhook handler in a local Node process.

target.title: fix(acp): persist spawn labels in target session store
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` persisted the spawned ACP session metadata into the target agent store, but tried to persist the label into the requester store.
- Why it matters: Cross-agent ACP spawns (for example from a Telegram topic into a `codex` ACP session) silently lost the requested label, so follow-up commands like `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` could not resolve the spawned session by label.
- What changed: `persistSpawnedSessionLabel(...)` now resolves the target session store from the spawned ACP `sessionKey` before writing the label, and only mutates the in-memory requester store when both stores are actually the same.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): ACP spawn routing, binding behavior, and runtime option handling are unchanged.
- AI-assisted: Yes. This PR was prepared with Codex.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ACP sessions spawned with `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` now retain the requested label even when the spawned session is written to a different agent session store than the requester. Label-based follow-up commands such as `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` now work in those cross-agent ACP flows.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
- New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No
- Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local source install + local dev checkout
- Model/provider: ACPX (`codex` / `gemini` harnesses)
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram ACP topic binding
- Relevant config (redacted): `acp.enabled=true`, `acp.backend=acpx`, `acp.allowedAgents=["codex","gemini"]`

### Steps

1. From a Telegram topic with ACP commands enabled, run `/acp spawn codex --bind here --label codex-a2ui --cwd /path/to/worktree`.
2. Observe that the spawned ACP session is created in the `codex` session store.
3. Try to resolve the session by label, for example with `/focus codex-a2ui` or `/acp status codex-a2ui`.

### Expected

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store includes `label: "codex-a2ui"`.
- Label-based session resolution works for follow-up commands.

### Actual (before fix)

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store had `label: null`.
- The label was written against the requester store instead, so label-based resolution failed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: local reproduction showed the spawned ACP session persisted in the target store with `label: null` before the fix; after patching the persistence path, the same session entry carried the requested label.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: traced the spawn path, confirmed the label write used the requester `storePath`, reproduced the bug locally with a Telegram-bound ACP `codex` session, then verified the patched code writes to the target session store resolved from the spawned `sessionKey`.
- Edge cases checked: confirmed the in-memory requester store is only mutated when it matches the resolved target store, so same-store spawns still behave normally.
- What you did **not** verify: kept verification minimal per request. This checkout's current `origin/main` also has unrelated missing-dependency/typecheck failures (`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `matrix-js-sdk`, and related surfaces), so `pnpm build` and the focused `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts` run did not complete cleanly in this environment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
- Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
- Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: labels still missing on cross-agent ACP spawns, or label writes regressing for same-store spawns

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The fix touches session-store selection during spawn label persistence, so a bad store-path resolution could break label writes for a subset of ACP spawn flows.
  - Mitigation: Added a regression test that specifically covers a requester-store/target-store mismatch and asserts the label is written to the spawned session store, not the requester store.

target.title: feat(plugins): add guard delivery and subagent review hooks
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the current hook surface can gate tool execution, but it cannot synchronously guard tool results before the agent sees them and it has no explicit pre-spawn / pre-return subagent review phases.
- Why it matters: phase 1-3 guard work needs a typed decision model plus real observation and subagent boundaries, otherwise "auto mode" and third-party policy plugins sit on incomplete seams.
- What changed: added shared guard decision/annotation types, new `before_tool_result_deliver` / `before_subagent_spawn` / `before_subagent_result_deliver` hooks, wired them into tool-result persistence and subagent spawn/announce flows, and extended targeted tests.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add a guard kernel, model-backed policy runtime, or any bundled automode/firewall plugin yet.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): N/A
- Why this regressed now: N/A
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/plugins/wired-hooks-subagent.test.ts`, `src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.tool-result-persist-hook.test.ts`, `src/agents/sessions-spawn-hooks.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-announce.test.ts`, `src/agents/pi-tools.before-tool-call.integration.e2e.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: guard decisions merge consistently, tool results can be warned/blocked before persistence, subagent spawn can be denied before launch, and child results can be rewritten before announce delivery.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests hit the exact hook seams without needing the full policy stack.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing hook wiring tests cover the older lifecycle hooks only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins can now return typed guard decisions and annotations from `before_tool_call`.
- Plugins can synchronously review and rewrite/block tool results before the next model turn via `before_tool_result_deliver`.
- Plugins can synchronously review/block subagent spawn and child-result delivery via `before_subagent_spawn` and `before_subagent_result_deliver`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees result]
[subagent spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [subagent announce]

After:
[tool call] -> [before_tool_call] -> [execute] -> [before_tool_result_deliver] -> [tool_result_persist] -> [agent sees guarded result]
[subagent spawn] -> [before_subagent_spawn] -> [subagent_spawning] -> [child runs] -> [before_subagent_result_deliver] -> [subagent announce]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: new hook phases can block or rewrite execution/results, but they only extend existing plugin authority. Mitigation is typed decision shapes, synchronous ordering, and targeted seam coverage around the new hook paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local repo shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default local repo config

### Steps

1. Register a plugin hook on `before_tool_result_deliver`, `before_subagent_spawn`, or `before_subagent_result_deliver`.
2. Run the corresponding tool/subagent flow.
3. Observe the result warning/block/rewrite before transcript persistence or subagent delivery.

### Expected

- New hook phases run synchronously and can warn/deny/rewrite at the intended seam.

### Actual

- Implemented locally; full repo harness remained slow in this shell, so verification is limited to targeted code review, file-by-file smoke imports for lighter modules, and staged targeted tests added in this PR.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: reviewed all new hook registration types and runner wiring, confirmed new hook names are in the public hook union/list, confirmed spawn/result guard call sites are wired, confirmed branch/commit contains the targeted tests.
- Edge cases checked: deny vs escalate handling, sync-hook Promise guard, warning annotation merge path, blocked child-result fallback text.
- What you did **not** verify: full `pnpm test` / `pnpm tsgo` completion in this shell; bounded `vitest` and `tsc` invocations timed out without surfacing a specific failure.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: plugins may start relying on the new guard decision semantics before a stronger core reducer/guard kernel exists.
  - Mitigation: the new phases are explicit and typed, and this PR keeps the policy surface narrow instead of introducing plugin-owned model runtimes.
- Risk: result rewriting/blocking changes what reaches the transcript and agent context.
  - Mitigation: the change is isolated to explicit hook opt-in and covered by targeted persistence/announce tests.

target.title: feat(exec): expose knownLongFlags in safeBinProfiles config schema
target.body:
## Summary

Expose the `knownLongFlags` field in the `safeBinProfiles` config schema, allowing operators to declare long flags as "known" for GNU abbreviation resolution without misusing `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`.

Closes #56805

> **Rebased 2026-06-10.** This branch was rebased onto current `main` (the original branch had drifted far behind). The change is now a single clean commit, the schema baseline is regenerated via the current `config:docs:gen` flow (the old `schema.base.generated.ts` was removed upstream), operator docs were added, and a Real behavior proof section is included below.

## Problem

The `knownLongFlags` field exists internally in `SafeBinProfile` and is used by `validateSafeBinArgv` for GNU long-option abbreviation resolution. However, it is not present in:

1. The Zod schema (`ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema`) — `.strict()` rejects it as an unknown key
2. The `SafeBinProfileFixture` type — no way to pass it through config normalization

For empty profiles `{}`, `knownLongFlags` auto-derives as `[]`, causing **all** long flags to be treated as unknown and rejected. The only workaround is to list every possible long flag in `allowedValueFlags` or `deniedFlags`, which changes their semantics.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags: z.array(z.string()).optional()` to `ToolExecSafeBinProfileSchema` |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy-profiles.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to `SafeBinProfileFixture`; normalize it in `normalizeSafeBinProfileFixture`; merge user-provided with auto-derived flags in `compileSafeBinProfile`, filtering strict prefixes of denied flags |
| `src/config/normalize-exec-safe-bin.ts` | Add `knownLongFlags` to the type cast in normalization |
| `docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md` | Document the field, its auto-derivation default, and the prefix-filter security semantics |
| `docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256` | Regenerated via `pnpm config:docs:gen` for the new field |
| `src/infra/exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` | 8 new tests for the config path + security filter |

## Design Decisions

- **Merge, don't replace**: User-provided `knownLongFlags` are merged with auto-derived flags from `allowedValueFlags ∪ deniedFlags`, so flags in those lists stay "known" even if not explicitly listed.
- **Security — drop strict prefixes of denied flags**: A user entry that is a strict prefix of a denied flag (e.g. `--rec` when `--recursive` is denied) is filtered at compile time, so it cannot gain exact-match priority in `resolveCanonicalLongFlag` and bypass the denial. (Addresses the prior Codex review P1.)
- **Reuses `normalizeFixtureFlags`**: Same normalization (trim, dedupe, sort) as other flag arrays.
- **Backwards-compatible**: Existing configs without `knownLongFlags` work identically via auto-derivation.

## Usage

```json5
{
  tools: {
    exec: {
      safeBins: ["mycli"],
      safeBinProfiles: {
        mycli: {
          allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"],
          deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
          knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"],
        },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Setting `tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.<bin>.knownLongFlags` in a real config previously could not be accepted — the strict Zod schema had no such key. After this patch the field is accepted by config validation and is honored by the safe-bin argv validator (declared long flags resolve, including GNU abbreviation), while strict prefixes of denied flags are still blocked.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw build from this branch, `OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (bb6e477)`, Node v22.22.0, run via the real `openclaw` CLI and the compiled `exec-safe-bin-policy` module (not mocks).

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
# 1) Real CLI accepts a config containing knownLongFlags
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof.json5 openclaw config validate --json
# config: { tools: { exec: { safeBins: ["mycli"], safeBinProfiles: {
#   mycli: { allowedValueFlags: ["--limit"], deniedFlags: ["--recursive"],
#            knownLongFlags: ["--dry-run", "--verbose"] } } } } }

# 2) Same CLI still rejects an unknown key (the pre-fix state of knownLongFlags)
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5 openclaw config validate --json
#   ( mycli: { knownLongFlagsTYPO: ["--dry-run"] } )

# 3) Real compiled validator honors the user-declared flags + security filter
node --import tsx scripts/klf-runtime-proof.mts
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```text
# step 1 — openclaw config validate (knownLongFlags present)
{"valid":true,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof.json5"}

# step 2 — openclaw config validate (unknown key knownLongFlagsTYPO)
{"valid":false,"path":"/tmp/klf-proof-unknown.json5",
 "issues":[{"path":"tools.exec.safeBinProfiles.mycli","message":"Invalid input"}]}

# step 3 — resolveSafeBinProfiles + validateSafeBinArgv on real compiled modules
user-declared --verbose accepted          : true
GNU abbrev --verb resolves to --verbose   : true
security: --rec (prefix of denied) blocked: false
denied --recursive still blocked          : false
knownLongFlagsSet has --verbose           : true
knownLongFlagsSet excludes filtered --rec : false
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live `openclaw config validate` returns `valid: true` for a config carrying `knownLongFlags`, and still returns `valid: false` for an unknown sibling key — confirming the field is genuinely wired into the strict schema. At runtime the compiled validator accepts the user-declared `--verbose`, resolves the `--verb` abbreviation to it, keeps `--recursive` denied, and drops the `--rec` prefix so it cannot bypass that denial.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end exec of a real third-party binary through the approval pipeline (requires a trusted-dir executable); behavior is covered at the policy/validator layer instead.

## Test Plan

- [x] All tests in `exec-safe-bin-policy.test.ts` pass: **98 passed** (90 existing + 8 new) under `vitest.infra.config.ts`
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` and `pnpm config:schema:check` pass (baseline in sync, no drift)
- [x] `oxlint` clean on changed files; no new type errors introduced
- [x] Real behavior proof captured above via the `openclaw` CLI and compiled validator

target.title: fix/agents-primary-model-selection-and-catalog-loading-41344
target.body: ## Summary - Fixes incorrect Primary Model selection when switching between agents in Agents > Overview. - Fixes partial / blank Primary Model dropdowns after refresh or first load by ensuring the model catalog is available on Agents Overview paths. - Adds targeted tests for both selection stability and catalog hydration behavior. - Keeps the fix scoped to the Agents Overview model-selection path. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Related #41344 - Related #46275 - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: 1. The agent details subtree could reuse stale DOM/select state when switching agents, which caused the Primary Model select to drift toward the previously viewed agent's state. 2. Agents Overview depended on a shared model catalog that was not guaranteed to be loaded on refresh / first-load / overview-only paths, so the select could render before the catalog was ready and degrade into a partial or blank-looking list. - Missing detection / guardrail: - Existing coverage covered some selected-value behavior, but it did not cover model-catalog hydration timing or the refresh/overview path where the catalog starts empty and arrives later. - Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): - Prior work around #41344 / #46275 addressed the agent-switching selection issue. Revalidating that fix on latest `upstream/main` exposed a second, related lifecycle issue in Agents Overview model-catalog loading. - Why this regressed now: - The Overview UI depends on model-catalog data, but not all entry paths guaranteed that the catalog was loaded before rendering. - If unknown, what was ruled out: - Ruled out a pure selected-state-only explanation. The partial/blank dropdown after refresh was caused by catalog availability/lifecycle, not just DOM selection drift. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should have caught this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.browser.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/views/agents.test.ts` - `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: - Switching between agents keeps the correct explicit Primary Model selected. - The inherit-default option text does not go blank. - When the model catalog is initially empty, Agents Overview shows a loading state instead of a misleading partial list. - When the catalog hydrates later, the full list appears without losing the selected value. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: - The bug spans both UI render behavior and shared model-catalog lifecycle, so a combination of focused controller/unit tests plus browser-level select behavior tests is the smallest reliable coverage. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): - Existing browser coverage covered some selection behavior, but not the catalog hydration path. - If no new test is added, why not: - N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). If none, write `None`. - Switching between agents no longer causes the Primary Model select to show the wrong agent's value. - On refresh / first entry to Agents Overview, the Primary Model select no longer degrades into a misleading partial list. - While the model catalog is still loading, the UI now shows a loading state instead of pretending only a small subset of options exists. - Once the catalog loads, the full option list appears without losing the current selection. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: - N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Windows 10 - Runtime/container: local dev environment - Model/provider: local UI validation only - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): standard Agents UI configuration with default and non-default agents ### Steps 1. Open Agents > Overview > Model Selection > Primary Model. 2. Switch between a default agent and a non-default agent. 3. Refresh the page and reopen the Primary Model select immediately. 4. Optionally wait for model-catalog hydration or switch away/back to Overview. ### Expected - The select reflects the correct explicit value for the current agent. - The inherit-default option text is stable. - Refresh / first-load does not show a misleading partial catalog. - Once the catalog is available, the full model list appears without losing the selected value. ### Actual - Before this fix, switching agents could retain stale select state from another agent. - Before this fix, refresh / first-load could show only fallback options (for example inherit/current) or appear blank until the catalog became available later. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ### UI Evidence #### Before After refresh / first load, the Primary Model dropdown could show only fallback entries or appear blank. #### After After the fix, the Primary Model dropdown restores the full option list correctly after refresh / first load, without losing the selected value. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Switched repeatedly between default and non-default agents and confirmed Primary Model stays tied to the current agent. - Refreshed the page and reopened Agents Overview to verify the select no longer falls into a misleading partial/blank state. - Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: - Multiple UI entry points now ensure model-catalog availability, which could increase repeated refresh attempts if not properly deduped. - Mitigation: - The loading/ensure logic is centralized in the shared model controller and covered by targeted tests. - Risk: - Keyed subtree remounting can discard unsaved local UI state when switching agents. - Mitigation: - That tradeoff is intentional here because correctness of agent-specific model selection is more important than preserving stale subtree state across agent switches.

target.title: fix(build): fall back to tsx for build TypeScript scripts
target.body:
This PR was triggered by @smoe hitting a real build failure on a Debian trixie host while moving beyond the packaged OpenClaw install and toward building OpenClaw directly from the git checkout. That host was running Debian’s Node.js 24 build, where `process.features.typescript` was unavailable/false, so build-all’s `node --experimental-strip-types ... .ts` step failed late in the build.

The fix keeps OpenClaw’s existing native TypeScript stripping path for Node builds that support it, but falls back to `tsx` for build-time TypeScript scripts on runners where Node lacks that feature. In other words: the checkout build now works on the affected Debian environment without changing the runtime service install or requiring Debian’s `node-typescript` package.

## Summary

- Fixes `scripts/build-all.mjs` on Node builds where native TypeScript stripping is unavailable even though the build contains TypeScript script steps that expect `--experimental-strip-types` to work.
- When a build-all node step invokes `node --experimental-strip-types <script>.ts`, the runner now checks `process.features?.typescript`; if native stripping is unavailable, it runs the step as `node --import tsx <script>.ts` instead.
- This matters because distro/custom/CI Node binaries can lack native TypeScript stripping support; in that environment the late `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` build step fails after earlier build work has already completed.
- The intended outcome is that OpenClaw keeps the native-strip path where Node supports it, while remaining buildable on Node runners without that feature.
- Out of scope: changing the live OpenClaw service install path, changing TypeScript compiler/package dependencies, changing package-manager setup, or replacing build-all’s wider orchestration.
- Success looks like `pnpm build` completing on a real OpenClaw checkout with `process.features.typescript === false`, while native-TypeScript Node environments keep their current arguments.
- Reviewers should focus on the fallback contract, the narrow trigger conditions, and the table-driven regression coverage for fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Linked context

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- Requested by an operator of an affected OpenClaw installation after a real build failed on their host.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `pnpm build` failed late in `scripts/build-all.mjs` when the `write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts` step attempted to run a TypeScript script through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary without native TypeScript stripping support.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw checkout on `gentle.functional.domains`, user `clawbio`, Node `v24.15.0`, with `process.features.typescript === false`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
  - `pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
- Evidence after fix:
  - Environment check output: `v24.15.0 typescript=false`
  - Current focused test result after addressing review feedback: `Test Files 1 passed (1)`, `Tests 39 passed (39)`
  - Full build result from the original patch session: `pnpm build` completed past the previously failing TypeScript-script step.
- Observed result after fix: the affected TypeScript script step is routed through `node --import tsx scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable, while `--experimental-strip-types` is preserved when Node reports TypeScript support.
- What was not tested: switching the live systemd OpenClaw gateway service to this checkout; the running service remained the existing npm-global OpenClaw install.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: proof is from one real Linux/OpenClaw host whose Node binary reports `process.features.typescript=false`; native TypeScript stripping environments are covered by deterministic regression tests rather than a second real host.
- Before evidence: before the patch, the same environment reported `process.features.typescript=false`, and `pnpm build` failed at the late `node --experimental-strip-types scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` build step.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node -p "process.version + ' typescript=' + process.features.typescript"`
- `/home/clawbio/.npm-global/bin/pnpm test test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`
- `pnpm build` during the original patch verification session

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Added table-driven coverage for TypeScript node build steps being rewritten to `--import tsx` when native TypeScript stripping is unavailable.
- Added coverage that extra script arguments are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.
- Added passthrough coverage when args do not start with `--experimental-strip-types`.
- Added passthrough coverage when the strip-types target is not a `.ts` file.
- Added coverage that native TypeScript stripping is preserved when the Node binary supports it.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- `pnpm build` failed late in build-all when running `scripts/write-plugin-sdk-entry-dts.ts` through `node --experimental-strip-types` on a Node binary with `process.features.typescript=false`.

If no test was added, why not?

- A focused regression test was added in `test/scripts/build-all.test.ts`.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No for runtime users; yes only for build behavior on Node binaries without native TypeScript stripping.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

- No.

What is the highest-risk area?

- Build command selection for TypeScript scripts in build-all.

How is that risk mitigated?

- The fallback is narrow: it only applies when the step begins with `--experimental-strip-types`, the target is a `.ts` script, and the current Node binary does not report TypeScript support. Existing native-strip behavior is preserved when `process.features.typescript` is available. The compatibility constraint is documented inline above the helper, and regression tests cover fallback, passthrough, extra args, and native-strip preservation.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Maintainer review of the narrow build-all fallback, inline compatibility comment, and regression tests.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- Waiting on maintainer review and CI. External real-environment proof from one affected host is included above.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- Addressed Claude review feedback from @smoe’s local review pass:
  - added the required inline comment explaining the fallback constraint;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-strip args;
  - added passthrough coverage for non-`.ts` targets;
  - added coverage that extra args are forwarded through the `tsx` fallback.

target.title: feat(slack): add ignoreOtherMentions channel config
target.body:
## Summary

- Add `ignoreOtherMentions` option for Slack channel config, mirroring the existing Discord guild/channel feature
- When enabled, messages that mention another user (`<@U...>`) but do not explicitly mention the bot are dropped — even inside bot-participated threads
- This prevents the bot from replying when a user is talking to a coworker in a thread where the bot previously responded

## Slack vs Discord difference

Discord's `ignoreOtherMentions` checks `!wasMentioned && !implicitMention`. The Slack version intentionally omits the `!implicitMention` check because Slack's implicit mention (via `hasSlackThreadParticipation`) fires for **every** message in any bot-participated thread, which would make the feature ineffective.

## Config example

```json5
{
  channels: {
    slack: {
      channels: {
        "*": { ignoreOtherMentions: true },
      },
    },
  },
}
```

## Changed files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/types.slack.ts` | Add `ignoreOtherMentions` to `SlackChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-core.ts` | Add zod validation for the new field |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/channel-config.ts` | Wire through type + resolver |
| `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.ts` | Drop logic before mention gating |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm build` passes
- [x] `pnpm test -- extensions/slack/src/monitor` — 236 tests pass
- [x] Manual test on live Slack workspace: bot no longer replies when tagging a coworker in a bot thread
- [x] Bot still replies when explicitly @mentioned in the same thread

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat: expose rawBody on user messages in plugin hook events
target.body:
### Summary

Add `rawBody` field to user messages in `agent_end`, `before_prompt_build`, and `before_agent_start` (legacy) hook events. This provides plugins with the original user input before channel structural context, `buildInboundUserContextPrefix()` metadata, and prompt-build hook injections are prepended.

### Motivation

Plugin hooks receive `event.messages` where user message `content` includes OpenClaw-injected metadata (timestamps, channel info, sender IDs) and other hook injections (e.g., memory context). Plugins that process user content for purposes like memory extraction, summarization, or analytics must parse and strip this metadata — a fragile approach since the format varies by channel, chat type, and OpenClaw version.

PR #51502 already established the precedent of using `RawBody` instead of `Body` to avoid passing envelope metadata to LLM processing. This PR extends that principle to plugin hook events.

### Changes

- Compute `rawBodyForHooks` from `CommandBody ?? RawBody ?? Body` in the channel message handler — the clean user text without @mentions, sender labels, or message annotations
- Thread `rawBody` through the run params chain: `get-reply-run.ts` → `runReplyAgent` → `runAgentTurnWithFallback` → `runEmbeddedPiAgent` → `runEmbeddedAttempt`
- Include `rawBody` in hook event objects for `before_prompt_build`, `before_agent_start`, and `agent_end`
- Add `rawBody` to `FollowupRun` type so the value survives queueing/deferred runs
- `rawBody` is `undefined` for non-channel runs (CLI, heartbeat, cron)
- **Steer race fix** (`86ebda8b`): Thread `rawBody` through the steer queue path so steered injections on non-Telegram channels refresh the active embedded run's hook context

### Backward Compatibility

- Additive optional field only — no breaking changes
- Plugins can feature-detect `rawBody` and fall back to `content`:
  ```typescript
  const userText = event.rawBody ?? extractText(lastUser.content);
  ```
- Plugin SDK API baseline unchanged (no drift)

### Use Case

Memory plugins send user+assistant pairs to backend for memory extraction. Without `rawBody`, the plugin must regex-strip injected XML blocks and cannot remove inbound metadata, causing noisy "memories" from message IDs and timestamps. With `rawBody`, the plugin gets clean user input directly.

### Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Plugin hook events (`before_prompt_build`, `agent_end`) now expose `event.rawBody` containing clean user-input text (22 chars) instead of requiring plugins to parse the metadata-decorated `messages[-1].content` (411 chars). Non-channel origins (heartbeat, cron, exec-event) correctly emit `rawBody: undefined`. Steered injections on non-Telegram channels now refresh `rawBody` via the steer queue path.

Real environment tested: Linux 6.17.0-1013-aws (arm64), Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.20, Bedrock `global.anthropic.claude-opus-4-7` (us-east-1, IAM role auth), Telegram bot `@oc_jmu_bot` via long-poll, custom probe plugin (`rawbody-probe`) using `definePluginEntry` + `api.on("before_prompt_build")` + `api.on("agent_end")`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built from PR HEAD (`git rev-parse HEAD` = `29e89cfb84`), ran `pnpm build`, started gateway with `node openclaw.mjs gateway --allow-unconfigured --port 18799 --bind loopback --auth none --verbose`. Sent Telegram DM "hello rawbody test 004" to `@oc_jmu_bot`. Triggered heartbeat via `node openclaw.mjs system event --text "..." --mode now --url ws://127.0.0.1:18799 --token anonymous --json`. Ran unit tests: `pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts -t "rawBody hook gating" --reporter=verbose`.

Evidence after fix:
```
Case A — Channel happy path (Telegram DM):
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 4c1c5a9b-8e2c-405f-b6f3-d2246e4912cc
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = user
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = "hello rawbody test 004"   (22 chars)
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "Conversation info (untrusted metadata):\n...
                            hello rawbody test 003"   (411 chars)
[rawbody-probe] equal    = false
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = prompt,messages,rawBody

Case B — Heartbeat origin:
[rawbody-probe] runId    = 40d31407-6882-4f23-bdc7-85ba18fb5a41
[rawbody-probe] trigger  = heartbeat
[rawbody-probe] rawBody  = <undefined>
[rawbody-probe] lastUser = "[OpenClaw heartbeat poll]"
[rawbody-probe] eventKeys = messages,success,error,durationMs,rawBody,runId

Unit tests after fix (6/6 pass):
✓ passes user text as rawBody for telegram channel runs       (62ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for heartbeat runs               (2ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for cron-event runs              (1ms)
✓ gates rawBody to undefined for exec-event runs              (1ms)
✓ prefers CommandBody over Body for channel rawBody           (1ms)
✓ threads rawBody through steer path for non-telegram channels (3ms)
Test Files  1 passed
     Tests  6 passed
```

Observed result after fix: `event.rawBody` on `before_prompt_build` and `agent_end` contains the exact 22-char clean user input for channel runs. Non-channel origins correctly emit `undefined`. The steer path unit test confirms `followupRun.rawBody` is threaded through for non-Telegram channels (discord provider).

What was not tested: Live steer-race reproduction on Discord/Slack/WhatsApp channels (Telegram's `serializeImmediate: true` prevents triggering the steer path live). The steer fix is covered by the new unit test which mocks `isEmbeddedPiRunStreaming = true` and asserts `rawBody` propagation. No multi-user concurrency test was performed.

target.title: Plugins: enforce private import boundaries
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Plugins could couple directly to private OpenClaw internals or escape their package root through imports, even though the intended contract is the plugin SDK seam.
- Why it matters: That makes plugin behavior depend on unstable host internals and weakens the architecture boundary between plugin code and core.
- What changed: Added an enforced loader-time import policy that allows `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*`, declared package dependencies, Node builtins, and in-package relative imports, while blocking private `openclaw/*` imports, undeclared bare packages, and imports that escape the plugin root.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This does not add process isolation or a sandbox; plugins still run arbitrary JS in-process.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Third-party and bundled plugins now fail to load when they import private OpenClaw package paths, undeclared bare packages, or files outside their own package root.
- Plugin authors can continue using declared dependencies, Node builtins, and `openclaw/plugin-sdk/*` seams.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - Plugin code can no longer import private OpenClaw internals or escape its package root through the loader import path. This is contract hardening, not malicious-plugin sandboxing.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): plugin loader
- Relevant config (redacted): local temp plugin fixtures

### Steps

1. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/runtime` or `../outside.cjs`.
2. Load it through the plugin loader.
3. Create a plugin that imports `openclaw/plugin-sdk/core`, a declared dependency, and an in-package helper.

### Expected

- Private OpenClaw imports and root-escaping imports are rejected before plugin execution.
- Declared dependencies, SDK seams, and in-package helpers continue to load.

### Actual

- Matches expected in direct local probes.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:

## Summary

- Problem: Chat channels did not support listing or switching between prior sessions under the same SessionKey, and follow-up fixes were needed to preserve switchable transcripts until LRU eviction and restore per-session usage/compaction snapshots correctly.
- Why it matters: Without in-chat session switching, users cannot navigate past conversations from Feishu/DingTalk/Telegram/Discord-style chat surfaces; incorrect transcript/archive or snapshot behavior would also make `/session back`, `/sessions`, and `/status`-adjacent state feel unreliable.
- What changed: Added `/sessions`, `/session <number>`, `/session <sessionId>`, and `/session back` for chat channels; introduced per-SessionKey LRU `sessionHistory`; preserved switchable transcripts until history eviction; restored token/context/compaction snapshots on switch; documented matching/order/retention behavior; cleaned up reset semantics so `session.historyLimit=0` truly disables switchable history.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not change SessionKey routing, does not add cross-SessionKey switching, does not add named sessions/UI picker flows, and does not change channel plugin-specific transport behavior.

target.title: fix(security): use constant-time comparison for TLS fingerprint verification
target.body:
## Summary

TLS certificate fingerprint verification in `src/gateway/client.ts` uses `!==` (standard JavaScript string equality), which is vulnerable to **timing side-channel attacks** (CWE-208).

An attacker on the network path can measure response time differences to iteratively guess the correct fingerprint byte-by-byte, potentially bypassing TLS pinning for MITM attacks on gateway connections.

## Root cause

Two locations perform fingerprint comparison with `!==`:

| Location | Context |
|----------|---------|
| Line 205 | WebSocket `checkServerIdentity` callback |
| Line 681 | HTTP `validateTlsFingerprint` method |

## Fix

Replace both with `safeEqualSecret()` from `src/security/secret-equal.ts`, which uses `SHA-256` hashing + `crypto.timingSafeEqual` for constant-time comparison.

This is the **same function already used** for pairing token verification elsewhere in the codebase — just missed for TLS fingerprints.

```diff
- if (fingerprint !== expected) {
+ if (!safeEqualSecret(fingerprint, expected)) {
```

## Why safeEqualSecret and not raw timingSafeEqual

`timingSafeEqual` requires both buffers to have equal length, which would leak the length of the expected fingerprint. `safeEqualSecret` avoids this by hashing both values with SHA-256 first (producing fixed 32-byte digests), then comparing the digests in constant time.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — compiles cleanly
- [x] `pnpm check` — lint/format passes
- [x] Existing `safeEqualSecret` tests in `src/security/audit-extra.sync.test.ts` cover equal, unequal, different-length, and undefined inputs
- [x] No remaining `fingerprint !== expected` patterns in codebase

allowed.

receiving account.

because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

target.title: Rescue: add watchdog core service and cron engine
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had no built-in isolated rescue watchdog that could detect an unhealthy managed gateway profile and repair it without going through the failing primary session.
- Why it matters: the larger rescue-watchdog feature is hard to review as one PR; this split lands the core runtime/service layer first so maintainers can review the actual watchdog mechanism without onboarding/UI noise.
- What changed: adds daemon/service hardening needed by the watchdog, introduces a new isolated `rescueWatchdog` cron payload + runner, and wires the gateway cron service to execute that watchdog job against a monitored profile.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no onboarding wizard flow, no CLI `openclaw onboard --rescue-watchdog` UX, and no docs changes in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #31480
- Supersedes #44113
- Supersedes #46493
- Supersedes #46499

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adds a new internal isolated cron payload kind, `rescueWatchdog`, for watchdog jobs that monitor a target profile and repair it when unhealthy.
- Hardens LaunchAgent plist writes: trusted-home resolution, symlink rejection, atomic writes, and `0600` plist permissions.
- Managed service restart helpers now accept `AbortSignal`, so watchdog restart attempts can be canceled cleanly instead of hanging until command timeouts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  The new capability is intentionally narrow: an isolated cron job can probe and repair a monitored local profile. Risk is bounded by restricting the payload shape, disallowing delivery targets for `rescueWatchdog`, resolving the monitored profile explicitly, and hardening the underlying managed-service restart and launchd plist write paths. The repair fallback uses exact argv via `process.execPath` and avoids shell-based PATH resolution.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm workspace
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): local managed gateway service / cron service only

### Steps

1. Run the daemon/unit tests covering launchd, systemd, schtasks, and process-exec timeout behavior.
2. Run the cron/rescue unit tests covering payload normalization, job validation, watchdog execution, and gateway cron wiring.
3. Build the repo and validate config to confirm the combined core layer compiles and loads cleanly.

### Expected

- A `rescueWatchdog` cron job can be normalized, stored, validated, and executed as an isolated job.
- Managed service restart helpers are abortable and launchd plist writes fail closed on unsafe targets.

### Actual

- Verified by targeted tests and local build/config validation; behavior matches the expected outcomes above.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - launchd plist path resolution ignores a conflicting `HOME` override, rejects symlink targets, and writes plist files with `0600` permissions via temp-file + rename.
  - `AbortSignal` now propagates through launchd/systemd/schtasks restart helpers into the process exec layer.
  - `rescueWatchdog` cron payloads normalize correctly, are forced to isolated session targets, and reject delivery/failureDestination config.
  - the rescue watchdog runner probes the monitored profile, attempts managed service restart, falls back to `doctor --repair --non-interactive`, and reports success/error summaries correctly.
- Edge cases checked:
  - `os.userInfo()` failure falls back to `os.homedir()`, and unresolved trusted home fails closed.
  - monitored rescue-profile names are rejected.
  - restart/repair flows respect cron timeout budget and abort behavior.
  - `launchd` temp plist files are cleaned up if rename fails.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - manual end-to-end watchdog repair on a real long-running host with an actually failing managed gateway.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR.
- Files/config to restore: `src/daemon/*`, `src/process/exec.ts`, `src/cron/*`, `src/rescue/watchdog-shared.ts`, `src/gateway/server-cron.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: launchd install unexpectedly failing on valid paths, or isolated cron jobs rejecting valid `agentTurn` jobs after the new payload kind was added.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: LaunchAgent path hardening could reject previously tolerated but unsafe filesystem layouts.
  - Mitigation: the checks are limited to symlinked/non-directory targets in the LaunchAgents path and have focused test coverage.
- Risk: adding a new cron payload kind could accidentally loosen validation or delivery behavior.
  - Mitigation: `rescueWatchdog` is explicitly restricted to isolated jobs, delivery is rejected, and schema/service/normalization paths have dedicated tests.
- Risk: watchdog repair code could leave stuck restart attempts behind.
  - Mitigation: abort propagation is threaded through the restart backends and the watchdog enforces bounded restart/repair timeouts.

target.title: security: create session dirs with private permissions
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories were created with default permissions, which landed as `0755` on POSIX hosts in practice.
- Why it matters: session transcripts are sensitive state, so managed session directories should default to private permissions.
- What changed: added `ensurePrivateSessionsDir(...)` for managed session directories and reused it across setup, onboarding, agent creation, and managed session store / transcript flows.
- Scope guard: caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories still use plain `mkdir`, while managed paths now reject symlinked final or ancestor components before permission hardening, including custom `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` roots.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are now created as private directories (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows). Caller-supplied custom session store / transcript directories keep their previous plain-`mkdir` behavior.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.6.1
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.0, pnpm 10.23.0
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): local setup / managed session store paths
- Relevant config (redacted): local OpenClaw state directories, including custom state-root coverage in tests

### Steps

1. Run setup or create a fresh managed session store under `agents/<id>/sessions/`.
2. Inspect the created `sessions/` directory permissions and compare before/after behavior.
3. Repeat with custom caller-supplied store / transcript paths to confirm they still use plain directory creation.
4. Exercise symlinked managed-path edge cases.

### Expected

- Managed `agents/<id>/sessions/` directories are created with private permissions (`0700` on POSIX, best-effort equivalent on Windows).
- Custom caller-supplied directories are not re-permissioned.
- Symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected before hardening.

### Actual

- Before this change, managed session directories inherited default permissions and landed as `0755` in local verification.
- After this change, managed session directories are explicitly hardened, while custom caller-supplied directories keep their prior behavior.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified locally:
  - `pnpm test -- src/config/sessions/paths.test.ts src/config/sessions/sessions.test.ts src/commands/setup.test.ts`
- Edge cases checked:
  - managed session directories are hardened to private permissions on POSIX
  - Windows permission assertions stay best-effort in tests
  - symlinked managed final or ancestor paths are rejected in the session-path helper tests
  - custom caller-supplied store / transcript directories are not forced to `0700`
- What you did **not** verify locally:
  - full repo `pnpm check`
  - full cross-platform onboarding E2E flows

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this PR's commit stack
- Files/config to restore: `src/config/sessions/paths.ts` and the managed session-directory callers
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: unexpected failures when managed session paths resolve through symlinked ancestors or unusual filesystems with restricted permission APIs

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: some filesystems may not honor the permission hardening path consistently.
  - Mitigation: the helper applies best-effort hardening and tests cover the intended managed-path behavior on supported platforms.
- Risk: broadening hardening to caller-supplied custom paths would be a behavioral regression.
  - Mitigation: custom store / transcript paths stay on plain `mkdir`; only managed session paths are hardened.

target.title: security: gateway public network hardening
target.body:
## Summary

Implements comprehensive security hardening for OpenClaw Gateway when exposed to public networks (e.g. `https://openclaw.allegro.earth/`). The existing security mechanisms (non-loopback auth enforcement, auth rate limiting, CORS checks) leave gaps that this PR addresses:

- **IP access control** (allowlist/blocklist with CIDR support) — enforced on both HTTP requests and WebSocket upgrades, loopback always allowed, blocklist takes priority
- **Password/token strength validation** — CRITICAL startup warnings when network-exposed with short tokens (<32 chars) or weak passwords (<12 chars, all-digit/all-letter patterns)
- **TLS enforcement check** — CRITICAL startup warning when network-exposed without TLS and no `terminatedUpstream` declaration
- **Per-IP request rate limiting** — sliding window (default 120 req/min/IP), loopback exempt by default, returns 429 with Retry-After
- **Auth audit logging** — JSONL to `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway-auth.jsonl` with file rotation, records `auth_failure`, `auth_success`, `rate_limited`, `ip_blocked` events
- **Auto HSTS** — `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000` set automatically when TLS is enabled
- **Eliminate silent 0.0.0.0 fallback** — `loopback`, `tailnet`, and `custom` bind modes now throw clear errors instead of silently falling back to all-interfaces
- **New security audit checks** — `gateway.no_tls_network_exposed` (critical), `gateway.password_too_short` (critical), `gateway.password_weak_pattern` (warn), `gateway.no_request_rate_limit` (warn), `gateway.auto_bind_fallback` (warn)
- **Enhanced `doctor` warnings** — TLS, password strength, token length, request rate limit checks for network-exposed gateways

### Config additions (`gateway.*`)

| Key | Type | Default | Description |
|-----|------|---------|-------------|
| `ipAllowlist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP allowlist (loopback always allowed) |
| `ipBlocklist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP blocklist (checked before allowlist) |
| `requestRateLimit.maxRequests` | `number` | 120 | Max requests per IP per window |
| `requestRateLimit.windowMs` | `number` | 60000 | Sliding window duration (ms) |
| `requestRateLimit.exemptLoopback` | `boolean` | true | Skip rate limiting for localhost |
| `tls.terminatedUpstream` | `boolean` | false | Declare TLS terminated by reverse proxy |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — type check passes
- [x] New unit tests: `ip-access-control.test.ts` (9 tests), `request-rate-limit.test.ts` (7 tests), `auth-audit-log.test.ts` (4 tests), `credential-strength.test.ts` (9 tests)
- [x] Existing tests updated and passing: `server-runtime-config.test.ts`, `doctor-security.test.ts`, `net.test.ts`, `auth.test.ts`, `audit.test.ts` — 245 tests total
- [ ] Manual: start gateway with `bind=lan` and verify startup warnings
- [ ] Manual: test IP blocklist returns 403
- [ ] Manual: test request rate limit returns 429

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat: configure metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, etc.) for custom provider setup
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets: - Problem: `openclaw configure --section model` sets up providers and auth but never prompts for model metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, reasoning, input types, cost). Users must hand-edit config or run many `config set` calls to configure these. - Why it matters: Custom/self-hosted endpoints default to contextWindow=16000 and maxTokens=4096, which are often wrong. Users have no interactive way to correct this during setup. - What changed: After custom provider setup in `promptAuthConfig`, the wizard now asks "Configure metadata for `/`?" and walks through the core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields (identity, capabilities, limits, cost) with current values prefilled. Built-in providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) are unaffected. Their catalog already provides defaults. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): The existing provider/auth flow is untouched. The metadata prompt is purely additive (appended after the custom provider path only). No config schema changes. No new CLI commands. Declining the prompt preserves the previous behavior exactly. Built-in provider flows are not modified. Compat flags (supportsStore, supportsTools, etc.) are not prompted for; they use sensible runtime defaults when absent and can be set manually in config by advanced users. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `openclaw configure --section model` with a **custom provider**: after provider setup completes, a new confirm prompt appears: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)". Answering Yes walks through all model metadata fields. Answering No preserves previous behavior. - **Built-in providers** (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.): no change. The metadata prompt does not appear. - Fields prompted (custom only): display name, API adapter, reasoning, input modalities, context window, max output tokens, token pricing (gated behind its own confirm). - All fields prefilled with current values; pressing Enter keeps defaults. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin 25.2.0) - Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm - Model/provider: Custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint ([thegrid.ai](https://thegrid.ai/)) - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): Standard `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with custom provider ### Steps 1. Run `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` 2. Select "Custom Provider" and complete auth/model setup 3. Observe the new prompt: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)" 4. Answer Yes and walk through the fields, or answer No to skip 5. (Optional) Repeat with a built-in provider (e.g. OpenAI) and confirm the metadata prompt does **not** appear ### Expected - After custom provider setup, a metadata configuration prompt appears - Answering Yes allows editing all core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields - Answering No skips metadata editing (same behavior as before this change) - Updated metadata is written to `models.providers..models[]` in config - Built-in providers skip the metadata prompt entirely ### Actual - Works as expected ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) 18 unit tests in `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` covering: `collectConfiguredModels`, `findModelInConfig`, `findPrimaryModelEntry`, `applyModelDefinitionUpdate`, `formatTokenCount`, `promptModelMetadata`, `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` (including skip for built-in providers). All passing. Existing `configure.wizard.test.ts` tests also pass. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Ran `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` end-to-end with a custom provider (thegrid-ai), confirmed metadata prompt appears after model setup. Ran with OpenAI, confirmed metadata prompt does **not** appear. - Edge cases checked: Declining metadata prompt preserves config unchanged; missing primary model gracefully skips; provider with no models returns empty list; built-in providers silently skip the prompt - What you did **not** verify: All built-in provider auth paths (only tested OpenAI). Other built-in providers use the same `authChoice !== "custom-api-key"` code path so are expected to behave identically. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes`: declining the new prompt produces identical config to before - Config/env changes? `No`: writes to existing `models.providers.*.models[]` fields - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the three-line addition in `configure.gateway-auth.ts` (lines 138-140: the `if (authChoice === "custom-api-key")` block calling `promptModelMetadataForPrimary`) - Files/config to restore: `src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.ts` (remove import + call), delete `src/commands/model-metadata.ts` and `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If the metadata prompt throws, it would interrupt the configure wizard after custom provider setup but before config write. The `WizardCancelledError` path is handled by the parent wizard. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Metadata prompt adds extra steps to the custom provider configure flow, potentially annoying for users who don't need it. - Mitigation: Gated behind a confirm ("Configure metadata?") defaulting to Yes but skippable with a single keypress. Cost is further gated behind its own confirm.

target.title: fix(skills): list omitted skill names when prompt is truncated
target.body:
## Problem

When many skills are installed (e.g. 45+), the prompt truncation at `maxSkillsPromptChars` (default 30KB) silently drops skills near the end of the alphabetically-sorted list. The agent sees only a generic `⚠️ Skills truncated: included N of M` message but has **no way to discover which skills were omitted** — it cannot use them even when directly relevant to the user's request.

Real-world example: a user sends a `mp.weixin.qq.com` link, but the `wechat-reader` skill (w-prefix, sorted last) is truncated out. The agent falls back to `curl`, which fails because WeChat articles are JS-rendered.

## Solution

When skills are truncated, the note now includes the **names** of omitted skills (capped at 1KB to stay compact) and suggests `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` so the agent can still discover and read them on demand.

**Before:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

**After:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit. Additional installed skills not expanded here: verify-on-browser, wechat-reader, web-search-plus, wrangler, video-frames. If a listed skill name becomes relevant, inspect it with `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` before deciding whether to read its SKILL.md.
```

## Changes

- `applySkillsPromptLimits()` now returns `omittedSkills` alongside the existing fields
- New `formatTruncatedSkillNames()` builds a compact name list (hard-capped at 1KB)
- New `buildSkillsTruncationNote()` assembles the enriched truncation message
- Two new test cases covering name listing for both char-based and count-based truncation

## Tests

All 34 existing + new tests pass (`npx vitest run src/agents/skills`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Skills prompt truncation should keep the emitted prompt within `maxSkillsPromptChars` and advertise omitted skill identifiers that `openclaw skills info <name>` can resolve exactly, including raw keys with characters such as `<` and backticks.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Darwin arm64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `fix/skills-truncation-visibility`, current HEAD `db926508c4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rendered 80 synthetic workspace skills through the real `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()` code path with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520` and raw key `skill-00<zero`raw`; then parsed the emitted `<omitted_skill_keys>` payload and resolved the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup. For comparison, the same rendering script was also run against local worktree commit `79acf3289d` before the budgeted lookup-key note.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local command run.

```text
Before (79acf3289d):
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (db926508c4):
{
  "commit": "after",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 512,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": true,
  "advertisedCount": 21,
  "advertisedFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw",
  "hasRawKey": true,
  "exactLookupFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw"
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

Observed result after fix: The current prompt is 512 chars, below the 520 char cap, includes `<omitted_skill_keys>`, preserves the raw advertised key as JSON data, and `formatSkillInfo()` resolves the first advertised key exactly as `skill-00<zero`raw`.

What was not tested: Live model submission was not needed for this prompt-rendering fix; the local proof covers the real prompt builder and CLI exact lookup formatter path.

Local script generated 80 workspace skills with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520`, injected the raw omitted lookup key `skill-00<zero`raw`, and rendered the same prompt path through `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()`. The after case also passes the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup.

Before (`79acf3289d`, pre budgeted lookup-key note):

```text
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
```

target.title: fix(ios): resolve main-thread warnings for CLLocationManager and SFSpeechRecognizer
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `GatewayConnectionController.currentPermissions()` triggered iOS UI-responsiveness warnings by repeatedly constructing `CLLocationManager` and querying speech authorization in a main-actor-sensitive way.
- Why it matters: repeated main-thread warnings are a signal of avoidable UI responsiveness risk in a frequently queried permission snapshot path.
- What changed: reused a single `CLLocationManager` instance, introduced a nonisolated speech-status accessor, and added tests around the updated permission snapshot behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no permission semantics changed, no gateway protocol changed, and no new device capabilities were introduced.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: locally built, device-tested, plus iOS unit-test coverage added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix

- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `currentPermissions()` performed main-thread-sensitive framework interactions in a way that recreated `CLLocationManager` per call and queried speech authorization directly on the main actor path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was not enough test/device validation focused on warning-free repeated permission snapshots.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): none required beyond the controller’s existing permission-snapshot implementation.
- Why this regressed now: iOS/Xcode warning surfaces became more visible/strict for these framework access patterns.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this was not a transport or gateway connection failure; it was a local controller/framework interaction warning.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `apps/ios/Tests/GatewayConnectionControllerTests.swift`
  - local device verification of warning-free permission snapshots
- Scenario the test should lock in: repeated permission snapshot collection should not emit the known main-thread warnings while preserving correct permission reporting.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover controller logic, while device verification confirms the warning symptom is gone in the real runtime.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): controller tests existed but were expanded because prior coverage did not assert the warning-prone path strongly enough.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- No direct UI feature change.
- Internally, permission snapshot collection is quieter and safer with respect to UI responsiveness warnings.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No

- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
