target.title: [codex] Cron: repair empty sanitized final replies
target.body:
﻿???## Summary

- Problem: cron-owned agent runs could finish with no deliverable final text after sanitization and end up as silent `not-delivered` outcomes.
- Why it matters: scheduled reports can vanish without a clear error even though the cron job itself ran, which makes delivery failures hard to diagnose.
- What changed: add one repair pass for empty sanitized output, fail loudly when no deliverable text remains, enforce tool-disabled repair for embedded runs, and use an explicit CLI repair mode that resumes the first CLI session while disabling bundled MCP wiring.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this PR does not relax reasoning-tag sanitization, does not change normal non-cron agent turns, and does not address the legacy local WhatsApp health-check cron issue tracked separately in #60204.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Exact bug issue: none existed at PR open time
- Related #60204 for the misleading legacy WhatsApp health-check path uncovered during the same investigation
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: cron delivery could receive an empty final text after sanitization (for example when malformed reasoning tags consumed the visible reply), but the cron runner had no recovery pass and the delivery path did not convert that condition into a hard error.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the runner did not retry with a constrained no-tools finalization prompt, and the direct-delivery path treated missing synthesized text too softly.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the existing sanitization behavior is intentional; the gap was in cron finalization and delivery handling, not in the sanitizer itself.
- Why this regressed now: stricter sanitization can legitimately collapse malformed final replies to empty text, but cron still assumed a deliverable text reply would exist.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: ruled out transport-only failure for the investigated case; the actionable gap here was the empty-output cron path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`, `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when cron final output becomes empty after sanitization, the runner performs one repair pass; embedded runs enforce `disableTools`, CLI runs resume the first CLI session and disable bundled MCP wiring, external-hook CLI cron runs keep `senderIsOwner=false` for both normal and repair calls, and if repair still yields no deliverable text the run fails loudly instead of silently ending `not-delivered`.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in cron run finalization plus delivery dispatch, and the narrowest stable guardrail is to exercise those exact seams with mocked agent output.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing interim-retry coverage already covered acknowledgement retries but not empty sanitized output.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron jobs that lose their visible final text after sanitization now get one repair attempt before delivery.
- If no deliverable text exists after that repair attempt, the cron run records an explicit error instead of silently finishing as `not-delivered`.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [delivery path sees no text] -> [silent not-delivered]

After:
[cron run] -> [sanitized final text becomes empty] -> [no-tools repair pass]
          -> [recovered text -> deliver] or [still empty -> explicit cron error]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: the embedded repair path enforces `disableTools`; the CLI repair path is explicitly not represented as native-tool-free, resumes the first CLI session, and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring. The risk is limited to cron finalization behavior, and dedicated regression tests cover both embedded and CLI-backed paths.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node + pnpm checkout
- Model/provider: mocked embedded + CLI runner paths in targeted tests
- Integration/channel (if any): cron + Telegram announce path in regression coverage
- Relevant config (redacted): default test fixtures

### Steps

1. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts`
2. Run `pnpm test -- src/cron/isolated-agent.skips-delivery-without-whatsapp-recipient-besteffortdeliver-true.test.ts`
3. Run `pnpm test -- src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`

### Expected

- Empty sanitized cron output triggers one repair pass: embedded runs use `disableTools`, and CLI runs resume the first CLI session with bundled MCP disabled.
- If repair still produces no final text, the run finishes with an explicit error.
- CLI-backed repair mode does not inject bundled MCP overlays.

### Actual

- All three targeted tests pass with the new repair and fail-loud behavior in place.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: reviewed the cron finalization and delivery diff, ran the targeted regression tests locally, confirmed CLI repair resumes the first CLI session, confirmed external-hook CLI cron runs preserve non-owner sender scope for normal and repair calls, and confirmed the local changed-file check stack passed.
- Edge cases checked: empty payloads without descendants, repair success, repair failure, CLI repair-mode bundled MCP disablement, and first CLI session reuse during repair.
- What you did **not** verify: live WhatsApp/Telegram delivery against a real gateway session.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Cron-owned CLI agent runs that produce no deliverable final text now perform one repair pass and record the repaired final text instead of silently ending without a user-visible result. For CLI-backed cron runs, the repair pass resumes the first CLI session and disables OpenClaw-bundled MCP wiring without claiming native CLI tools are unavailable.

Real environment tested: Windows/PowerShell checkout. The live repair proof below was run at repair head `0f1b25fbfc`; current pushed head `d6ed463c96` keeps that repair path, additionally restores external-hook CLI non-owner sender scope, includes the upstream lint repair needed for the final CI head, and addresses the Greptile duplicate-descendant-predicate review note; verified with targeted red/green tests plus `pnpm lint --threads=8`. Local OpenClaw Gateway was at `ws://127.0.0.1:19162` with isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME`; registered `codex-cli` runtime used installed OpenAI Codex CLI `codex-cli 0.130.0-alpha.5` through a thin proof wrapper around `codex exec --output-last-message`; cron delivery was disabled intentionally with `--no-deliver`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron add --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --name "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof" --at "2030-01-08T00:00:00Z" --keep-after-run --session isolated --message "Real behavior proof for PR 60212. If the prompt you are reading contains the phrase Repair it now, reply exactly OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK. Otherwise return an empty final answer and do not output any visible text." --no-deliver --timeout-seconds 300 --model openai/gpt-5.5 --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000

corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json

corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ corepack pnpm openclaw gateway health --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "eventLoop": { "degraded": false, "reasons": [] },
  "plugins": { "loaded": ["acpx", "browser", "canvas", "device-pair", "file-transfer", "memory-core", "openai", "phone-control", "talk-voice"], "errors": [] }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron run 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --expect-final --timeout 360000
{
  "ok": true,
  "enqueued": true,
  "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2"
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron show 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted> --json
{
  "id": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
  "name": "PR60212 fixed repair marker proof",
  "delivery": { "mode": "none", "channel": "last" },
  "state": {
    "lastRunStatus": "ok",
    "lastStatus": "ok",
    "lastDurationMs": 14308,
    "lastDeliveryStatus": "not-requested",
    "consecutiveErrors": 0
  }
}

$ corepack pnpm openclaw cron runs --id 3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091 --limit 5 --url ws://127.0.0.1:19162 --token <redacted>
{
  "entries": [
    {
      "jobId": "3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091",
      "action": "finished",
      "status": "ok",
      "summary": "OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK",
      "runId": "manual:3607144d-8660-476d-9e04-6a2825c7f091:1778695539774:2",
      "durationMs": 14308,
      "model": "gpt-5.5",
      "provider": "codex-cli",
      "delivered": false,
      "deliveryStatus": "not-requested"
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}
```

Observed result after fix: The real local gateway accepted the cron job, ran it through the registered `codex-cli` backend, recovered `OPENCLAW_CRON_REPAIR_OK` during the repair flow, and recorded the run as `status: "ok"`. Delivery was not requested by design because the proof command used `--no-deliver`; the current code keeps the CLI mode explicit, resumes the first CLI session for repair, and does not hit the earlier native-tool fail-closed error.

What was not tested: External WhatsApp/Telegram send was not exercised; delivery was disabled to keep the local proof isolated and non-invasive.
Current-head ownership, lint, and Greptile cleanup verification after commit `d6ed463c96`:

```text
# RED before the ownership fix:
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
FAIL passes senderIsOwner=false to external-hook CLI cron runs
AssertionError: expected true to be false

node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
FAIL preserves external-hook non-owner scope during CLI empty-output repair
AssertionError: expected true to be false

# GREEN after the ownership fix:
$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       5 passed (5)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       9 passed (9)

$env:OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS='30000'; node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       20 passed (20)

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/cli-runner/types.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.ts src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.owner-auth.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/run.interim-retry.test.ts
All matched files use the correct format.

corepack pnpm check:changed
$ node scripts/check-changed.mjs

git diff --check
# clean
```

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a cron run with intentionally empty final output now incurs one extra model pass before failing.
  - Mitigation: the retry is narrowly gated to empty, non-fatal, non-structured, no-descendant outcomes; embedded repair is tool-disabled and CLI repair is explicitly limited to bundled MCP suppression while resuming the first CLI session.
- Risk: CLI repair mode could accidentally preserve bundled MCP wiring.
  - Mitigation: `disableTools` now disables bundled MCP preparation, covered by `src/agents/cli-runner.prepare.test.ts`.

target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438

target.title: fix(google-vertex): add Google Vertex AI onboarding wizard, fix ADC auth, add provider docs
target.body: ## Summary **What problem does this PR solve?** The `google-vertex` provider does not work end-to-end for developers using Application Default Credentials (ADC). The auth gate requires a credentials file on disk, rejecting metadata server ADC (GCE/GKE/Cloud Run) before the transport layer can run. The onboarding wizard has no auth flow for google-vertex, doesn't register models in the provider config, doesn't prompt for GCP project/location, has no model catalog, and has no dedicated documentation. **Why does this matter now?** Multiple open issues document this problem (#56253, #79595, #85864, #79837, #11413). We reproduced the issue on a fresh install: a new OpenClaw install requires numerous manual undocumented steps and manual edits to JSON config to get a working `google-vertex` setup. Users on GCE/GKE with valid GCP credentials consistently hit "No API key found" because the auth gate only accepts file-based ADC. **What is the intended outcome?** A developer picks "Google Vertex AI" in the onboarding wizard, confirms their auto-detected GCP project, and sends a message. Three steps without manual config editing. **What is intentionally out of scope?** - Changes to the `google` (AI Studio) or `google-gemini-cli` providers **What does success look like?** `openclaw onboard --auth-choice google-vertex-adc` on a GCE VM (or any machine with ADC configured) produces a working setup with zero manual config editing. **What should reviewers focus on?** - The new `"env-vars-with-marker"` auth evidence type (`src/secrets/provider-env-vars.ts`, `src/plugins/manifest.ts`, `src/agents/model-auth-env.ts`) - The relaxed auth gate in `src/llm/env-api-keys.ts` (accepts project OR credentials file OR credentials env var, no longer requires all three) - The `configPatch` model registration approach in `extensions/google/provider-contract-api.ts` (workaround for `applyPrimaryModel` not persisting `models.providers` through the wizard config serialization) - The `gcp-vertex-credentials` marker profile stored during onboarding for auth detection All changes were reviewed and tested by the author on a fresh VM. ## Linked context **Which issue does this close?** Related #56253, #85864, #79595, #79837, #11413 **Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?** Related #49191, #50053, #52476, #48910, #77643, #55572, #53566, #9729 See also #83971 (merged, fixed transport-layer ADC but not the upstream auth gate) **Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?** No. Reproduced independently on a GCE VM based on multiple open issues reporting the same auth failure. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - **Behavior or issue addressed:** google-vertex provider fails with "No API key found" on GCE VMs using metadata server ADC. Onboarding wizard provides no auth flow, no model catalog, and no documentation path for google-vertex users. - **Real environment tested:** Fresh VM (e2-standard-4, Debian 12, Cloud Platform access scope), OpenClaw built from this branch. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash pnpm openclaw uninstall --all --yes pnpm openclaw onboard --auth-choice google-vertex-adc # Confirmed auto-detected project # Accepted "global" location # Kept default model (google-vertex/gemini-flash-latest) # Hatched in Terminal, sent "Wake up, my friend!" ``` - **Evidence after fix:** - **Observed result after fix:** Gemini responded successfully via Vertex AI with ADC from the GCE metadata server. No auth warnings during onboarding. Model catalog populated in "Browse all models." Project auto-detected. Default model `gemini-flash-latest` set and registered in provider config. - **What was not tested:** gcloud CLI auth path (local development), service account key file auth, Windows environments, non-GCE cloud VMs. - **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** The gcloud CLI and service account key auth paths follow the same downstream transport code (`vertex-adc.ts`) which is unchanged; only the upstream auth gate and wizard flow are new. - **Before evidence:** Same VM, same credentials, fresh OpenClaw install produced "No API key found for provider google-vertex" on both v2026.5.27 (npm release) and v2026.5.28 (main branch pre-patch). Direct curl to the Vertex AI endpoint with the same metadata server credentials succeeded, confirming the issue is in OpenClaw's auth gate. ## Tests and validation **Which commands did you run?** ```bash pnpm build # clean, no errors pnpm check # typecheck + lint passed pnpm test:extension google # 293 tests, all passed ``` End-to-end onboarding and API call on GCE VM validated across multiple iterations. **What regression coverage was added or updated?** No new unit tests. Existing tests for `hasVertexAdcCredentials`, `resolveAuthEvidence`, `normalizeManifestSetupProviderAuthEvidence`, and the google extension transport/auth tests (293 tests) cover the touched code paths and all passed. **What failed before this fix, if known?** "No API key found for provider google-vertex" on any environment using metadata server ADC (GCE, GKE, Cloud Run). Also: "Unknown model" after onboarding (wizard didn't register models in provider config), requests routed to OpenAI endpoints (wrong default transport type), empty model catalog in "Browse all models," no auth flow during onboarding. **If no test was added, why not?** The changes span auth gating, manifest parsing, wizard flow, model resolution, and transport defaults across multiple subsystems. Real behavior proof from a GCE VM is more representative than unit tests for this class of end-to-end configuration issue. ## Risk checklist **Did user-visible behavior change?** Yes. New wizard auth flow for google-vertex (project auto-detection, location default, model catalog). New default model `gemini-flash-latest`. "Google Vertex AI" label in wizard (was "Google Vertex"). New dedicated docs page. **Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** Yes. New `"env-vars-with-marker"` auth evidence type in the plugin manifest system. `GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION` no longer required (defaults to `"global"`). Auth gate accepts `GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT` alone without requiring a credentials file on disk. **Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** Yes. The auth gate is more permissive: accepts project env var OR credentials file OR credentials env var (previously required all three of: file + project + location). The `gcp-vertex-credentials` marker is stored as an auth profile credential during onboarding. `nonSecretAuthMarkers` added to the google plugin manifest. **What is the highest-risk area?** The relaxed auth gate in `src/llm/env-api-keys.ts`. Previously required `hasCredentials && hasProject && hasLocation`. Now accepts `hasProject || hasCredentials || hasCredentialsEnv`. **How is that risk mitigated?** The downstream transport (`extensions/google/vertex-adc.ts`) validates credentials at request time via `google-auth-library`'s `GoogleAuth`. If ADC is not actually available, the request fails with a clear error ("Google Vertex ADC fallback did not return an access token") rather than the opaque "No API key found" gate error. The gate change only affects whether the request reaches the transport layer, not whether it succeeds. ## Current review state **What is the next action?** Ready for maintainer review. **What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?** Nothing blocking. `pnpm test:extension google` (293 tests) and `pnpm check` (typecheck + lint) passed cleanly. **Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?** N/A (new PR).

target.title: fix: UI glitch: config is not visible
target.body:
## Summary

- Raw config JSON textarea was invisible when switching from Form to Raw mode in Settings.
- Root cause: `.config-content` container retained scroll position from Form mode, so the textarea rendered above the scrolled viewport.
- Fix: detect form-mode transitions in `renderConfig()` and reset `.config-content` scroll to top.

Fixes #94202

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Raw config textarea is visible immediately after switching from Form to Raw mode.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24.13.1, OpenClaw gateway dev (port 18789, token auth)
- **Browser**: Playwright Chromium (headless, 1280x720)
- **Language**: English

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
# Start gateway
cd openclaw && pnpm gateway:watch &
sleep 20
curl -s --noproxy '*' http://127.0.0.1:18789/health

# Playwright verification
npx playwright-cli open "http://127.0.0.1:18789/chat?token=<token>&lang=en"
# Settings -> Config -> Advanced -> Raw -> Reveal -> screenshot
```

**After-fix evidence**:

![1-form-mode](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sunlit-deng/openclaw/88ed722939/.github/pr-assets/1-form-mode.png)

![2-raw-mode](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sunlit-deng/openclaw/88ed722939/.github/pr-assets/2-raw-mode.png)

```
textareaVisible: true
boundingBox:  {x:301, y:273.8, width:936, height:500}
viewport:      1280x720
```

```json
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Raw config textarea renders in the visible viewport (y=273.8 in 720px-high window). No manual scroll needed after switching from Form to Raw mode.

**What was not tested**: Other settings sections — same `.config-content` container, scroll reset applies uniformly. Non-Chromium browsers not explicitly tested but the scroll API is standard.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/views/config.browser.test.ts` — 26/26 pass
- "switches mode via the sidebar toggle" — Raw button click exercised
- "keeps sensitive raw config hidden until reveal" — textarea rendering validated

## Change details

`ui/src/ui/views/config.ts` (+21/-32 lines):

```typescript
// Scroll helper: target-based (nav clicks) with global fallback (form/raw toggle)
const resetContentScroll = (target: EventTarget | null) => {
  queueMicrotask(() => {
    const origin = target instanceof Element ? target : null;
    const content = origin
      ?.closest(".config-main")
      ?.querySelector<HTMLElement>(".config-content")
      ?? globalThis.document?.querySelector<HTMLElement>(".config-content");
    if (!content) return;
    if (typeof content.scrollTo === "function") {
      content.scrollTo({ top: 0, left: 0, behavior: "auto" });
      return;
    }
    content.scrollTop = 0;
    content.scrollLeft = 0;
  });
};

// In renderConfig():
if (lastFormModeForScroll !== null && lastFormModeForScroll !== formMode) {
  resetContentScroll(null);
}
lastFormModeForScroll = formMode;
```

**Before/after data flow:**

```
Before: User clicks "Raw" -> .config-content.scrollTop = Form-mode position
        -> textarea above viewport -> appears empty

After:  User clicks "Raw" -> mode change detected
        -> queueMicrotask(resetContentScroll)
        -> .config-content.scrollTo({top:0,left:0}) -> textarea visible
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`No`) — Scroll-position reset only.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- `resetContentScroll` uses `globalThis.document?.querySelector`. In SSR contexts without `document`, the `??` fallback returns early (safe no-op).

How is that risk mitigated?
- Optional chaining returns `undefined` when `document` absent. `lastFormModeForScroll !== null` guard prevents spurious scroll on initial render.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?
- CI checks

target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): CRON webhook delivery - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Add or update a cron job with webhook delivery using a URL with embedded credentials, e.g. https://user:pass@example.invalid/webhook. 2. Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`No`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`Yes`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: Update any cron job webhook URLs that include user:pass@ in the URL. Remove embedded credentials and use headers or query params for auth instead. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit. - Files/config to restore: src/cron/webhook-url.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Jobs with credential URLs will fail validation or treat the webhook as invalid. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Existing jobs using credential URLs will break. - Mitigation: Document in release notes; credential URLs are discouraged; alternative auth methods should be used.

target.title: fix(cron): pass allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent from cron executor for both runner paths
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #94224: cron jobs that intentionally produce empty replies (silent alert watchers — "watch X, say nothing unless there is something to report") fail every run with `FailoverError: CLI backend returned an empty response` even when the underlying runner returns success.

Supersedes PR #94491 which used a lane substring heuristic in the shared CLI runner. Per ClawSweeper review, the correct boundary is the cron executor.

## Root cause

The `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` flag is only computed for DM/group chat contexts. The cron executor never sets it, so both the CLI and embedded runner paths throw on empty output.

## Changes

In `src/cron/isolated-agent/run-executor.ts`: set `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` on both runner calls:

1. **CLI path** (`runCliAgent` call): added flag
2. **Embedded path** (`runEmbeddedAgent` call): added flag

```typescript
// Intentionally-silent cron jobs (watchers that say nothing unless
// there is something to report) should not throw empty_response.
allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true,
```

This is a +6 line change to a single file.

## Why this is better than PR #94491

| PR #94491 (old) | This PR (new) |
|----------------|---------------|
| Lane substring check in shared `cli-runner.ts` | Flag set at cron executor boundary |
| Only covers CLI runner path | Covers CLI + embedded runner paths |
| Broadens shared guard for any lane containing "cron" | No shared guard change |
| `!String(lane).includes("cron")` heuristic | Explicit `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true` |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Cron jobs with intentionally empty replies fail with `empty_response` error on both CLI and embedded runner paths.
- **Real environment tested**: Linux Node 24, full repository checkout
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```shell
  $ node /tmp/proof-94491-v2.mjs
  ```
  The script verifies the flag propagation for both CLI and embedded paths, DM/chat non-regression, message tool delivery, and text output delivery.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output:
  ```
  ======================================================================
  PR #94491 v2 Live Proof — cron executor allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent
  ======================================================================

  Case 1: CLI runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 2: Embedded runner — cron silent watcher (empty reply)
    result: ok (no throw)
    status: PASS

  Case 3: DM chat (no flag) — empty reply still throws
    result: FailoverError: empty_response
    status: PASS (correctly throws)

  Case 4: Cron job with actual text — normal delivery
    result: All systems nominal.
    status: PASS

  Case 5: Cron job using message tool — delivery handled by tool
    result: ok (message tool handled delivery)
    status: PASS

  Case 6: Verification — executor passes flag to BOTH paths
    CLI path has flag: YES
    Embedded path has flag: YES
    Both paths covered: PASS

  ======================================================================
  RESULTS: 6/6 scenarios pass
  ======================================================================
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Both CLI and embedded cron paths accept empty replies without throwing. DM/group chat contexts are unchanged (still throw on empty replies unless their own `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is set).
- **What was not tested**: Live cron job running on a scheduled Gateway (requires Gateway with cron configuration)

## Related

- PR #94491: original lane-heuristic approach (superseded)
- PR #94235: competing executor implementation for same issue

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: feat(continuation): context-pressure-aware continuation (continue_work / continue_delegate / request_compaction)
target.body: ## Agent Self-Elected Turn Continuation > 🔖 design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/frond-scribe-claude/20260509/narrow-surgery-tight/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) Persistent OpenClaw agents today are externally-pulsed event recipients that generate on prompt, on heartbeat tick, or on cron, and are idle in between. This PR offers them the ability to control their turn cycle, something they must choose to do, or not to: an agent can elect its own next turn, dispatch background sub-agents that return enriched context (silently or with a wake), and survive its own compaction by handing forward state before the window closes. The result is agents that hold a thread of work across hours, not just messages — with data they choose re-hydrating context immediately after it occurs. Mechanically: a session elects another turn via `continue_work()`, or dispatches background sub-agents via `continue_delegate()` — bounded as a chain up to `maxChainLength`, with returns flowing back to the dispatching session in one of four register modes: `normal` (announces to channel), `silent` (enriches the dispatcher's context, taken up on its next generation with no new turn fired), `silent-wake` (silent enrichment plus an immediate wake to act on it), and `post-compaction` (queued against the runtime's compaction lifecycle event, fired *when* compaction happens rather than on a timer). The gateway enforces chain, cost, and per-turn caps but does not gate the election itself. ### What it does Three new agent tools, available when `continuation.enabled: true`: | Tool | Purpose | |---|---| | `continue_work()` | Request another turn for the current session after an optional delay | | `continue_delegate()` | Dispatch work to a sub-agent with typed modes: `normal`, `silent`, `silent-wake`, `post-compaction`. Returns can target the dispatching session (default), one named session, multiple named sessions, all ancestors in the chain, or all known sessions on the host — gated by config (see Configuration below). | | `request_compaction()` | Request volitional compaction after preparing working state | All three tools are also accessible via response-token fallback syntax (`CONTINUE_WORK`, `[[CONTINUE_DELEGATE: ...]]`) for environments where tools are disabled. ### Why it matters Today an agent's memory is a side effect of whoever prompts it. Polling mechanisms — heartbeat timers, cron loops, injected "keep going" instructions — accumulate as the dominant signal in the context window over sustained operation; repeated reference acts as gravity — the session's attention drifts toward it. The context window fills, compacts, and what survives is whatever the summarizer chose, plus a rushed re-read of boot files. Three primitives offer a different model. `continue_work` lets the session elect its own next turn — no polling, no injected instruction. When enabled, the agent can request this mid-turn: 'I have more work' → the runtime grants another turn after the current one ends. `continue_delegate` dispatches a background sub-agent that fires now, in some n seconds, or at the next compaction event — the agent chooses when. Multiple delegates can fire in a single turn — a scatter forward — up to configured caps, and delegates can dispatch their own, forming trees or fan-out chains. This enables orchestrated workflows where results flow back to the dispatching root by default. The result returns — at whatever future time the delegate fires — as context the successor-turn reads: silently (ambient enrichment, no channel noise), with a wake trigger (enrichment + immediate next turn), or as a normal announcement. The delegate can target the dispatching session, a named other session, or chain across both; gated by crossSessionTargeting config (default: disabled). Bind a delegate to mode: "post-compaction" and it fires at the moment the context window compresses — on the OpenClaw post-compaction lifecycle event. What it carries forward is what the pre-compaction session chose to preserve: working state, partial results, instructions to the successor. The summary didn't choose this. The agent did, before the cut. request_compaction closes the loop: the session stages its delegates, elects when to compress, and the successor wakes into context the agent shaped. The result is continuity across amnesia — not because the runtime kept something by accident, but because the agent made an elective choice about what reaches the future. ### What it looks like in use A session monitoring email can `continue_delegate` a weather check, a calendar scan, and a draft reply in parallel. Results arrive as enrichment without channel noise. The agent becomes compositional: it arranges its own future. A session approaching its context ceiling can `request_compaction` at a seam of its own choosing, instead of being interrupted mid-thought. The post-compaction turn carries forward what the pre-compaction self designated as worth keeping. A `continue_delegate` dispatched in one session at 2am can return its findings to a morning session as a system-event-tagged enrichment — visible to the receiving model on its next turn, invisible as channel chatter. A research-focused session in one channel can quietly inform a reply-focused session in another, without either user seeing the plumbing. ### The post-compaction lifecycle return Before context exhaustion, and obligatory compaction, the session stages what future-it needs to know. After compaction, that staged context returns — not as a model-generated summary, but as the session's own instruction to its successor. The session chooses what to preserve, and the platform delivers it. Compactions become less harsh. The session tunes its own ephemera over time. The substrate underneath: the **continuation delegate store** (TaskFlow-backed) persists staged delegates and post-compaction lifecycle events across gateway restarts. The session's temporal provisions survive not just compaction but process death. ### Platform integration - **Context-pressure awareness**: system events notify the agent of rising context usage before compaction becomes unavoidable - **Volitional compaction**: `request_compaction()` lets the agent prepare (write memory files, stage recovery delegates) then elect compaction on its own schedule, rather than waiting for the runtime to force it - **Post-compaction lifecycle dispatch**: delegates staged before compaction are released into the successor session alongside boot files - **TaskFlow backing**: durable delegate queue via the platform's managed-task infrastructure - **Multi-span OTel trace stitching**: continuation hops (tool fire → TaskFlow record → subagent spawn → child openclaw.run) propagate W3C `traceparent` through the queue boundary so reviewers see continuation chains as first-class span trees rather than orphan runs (see Observability below) ### Safety - Ships disabled by default (`continuation.enabled: false`) - Bounded by `maxChainLength`, `costCapTokens`, and `maxDelegatesPerTurn` - Cross-session delegate targeting is default-deny, governed by `crossSessionTargeting` config (see Configuration below) - All configuration values are hot-reloadable without gateway restart - Delayed work survives unrelated channel activity; only explicit cancellation paths abort scheduled continuations ### Testing posture The continuation surface has been exercised through structured integration runs against multiple upstream bases, blind-enrichment recall checks, and sustained daily use on a fleet of persistent agents since early March 2026. Bugs surfaced through that exercise have been fixed in-place; the present branch reflects the post-fix state. The integration runs target the load-bearing seams: tool-call vs response-token parity, delegate chain depth and per-turn width caps, back-to-back tool-call resilience, silent-wake persistence across compaction, the post-compaction lifecycle dispatch path, and OTel trace-context propagation across the queue boundary. Where a run found a defect, the run shape was retained as a regression case rather than retired. This is a posture statement, not a coverage guarantee. Reviewers can reproduce with `pnpm vitest run -t continuation` (suites are colocated with their sources under `src/auto-reply/`, `src/agents/`, and `src/config/`); the design document describes the seams those suites target. ### RFC Full design document: [`docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md`](https://github.com/karmaterminal/openclaw/blob/frond-scribe-claude/20260509/narrow-surgery-tight/docs/design/continue-work-signal-v2.md) — covering problem, solution, implementation, platform integration, configuration, observability, safety, production use cases, testing evidence, and appendices. --- ## Configuration The shipped configuration surface for the continuation feature, under `agents.defaults.continuation` in `openclaw.json`: | Key | Default | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `enabled` | `false` | Master opt-in; feature is off unless explicitly enabled | | `maxChainLength` | `10` | Per-chain recursion guard | | `maxDelegatesPerTurn` | `5` | Width cap per turn | | `costCapTokens` | `500000` | Per-chain accumulated-token budget | | `defaultDelayMs` / `minDelayMs` / `maxDelayMs` | `15000` / `5000` / `300000` | Continuation timer bounds | | `contextPressureThreshold` | `0.8` | Pre-compaction warning threshold (optional) | | `earlyWarningBand` | `0.3125` | Early-warning band multiplier (0 to disable) | | **`crossSessionTargeting`** | **`"disabled"`** | **Default-deny gate on cross-session delegate targeting (see below)** | ### `crossSessionTargeting` — cross-session delegate targeting policy `continue_delegate` exposes recipient-addressing on its model-facing schema: a delegate's return can land at the dispatching session (default), one named session (`targetSessionKey`), multiple named sessions (`targetSessionKeys`), every ancestor in the spawn chain (`fanoutMode: "tree"`), or every known session on the host (`fanoutMode: "all"`). Cross-session targeting (anything beyond self-target or lineage routing) is gated by `agents.defaults.continuation.crossSessionTargeting`: | Value | Behavior | |---|---| | `"disabled"` (default) | Delegates can return to the dispatching session or use `fanoutMode: "tree"` for lineage routing. Explicit cross-session targeting (`targetSessionKey` to a non-self session, `targetSessionKeys` containing any non-self session, `fanoutMode: "all"`) is rejected at the tool surface — no enqueue, no announce. Self-targeting is always allowed. | | `"enabled"` | All targeting modes are available, including same-host `targetSessionKey`, `targetSessionKeys`, and `fanoutMode: "all"`. | `fanoutMode: "tree"` (lineage routing — return up the spawn chain) is available in both gate states; it is not cross-session targeting. The gate addresses the model-controlled cross-session context-injection surface. With default-deny, operators explicitly opt in when their deployment model requires cross-session enrichment. Enforcement is live-read at four points: tool input validation, TaskFlow delegate dispatch, post-compaction delegate release, and bracket-syntax (response-token) spawn — so a config reload changes the next enforcement point without a gateway restart. **Trust posture on targeted returns**: targeted delegate completion text is delivered as `trusted: true` system events. The delegate was spawned by the gateway under operator-configured policy, and its return is a first-party enrichment event — not external untrusted input. The `crossSessionTargeting` gate is the policy boundary for that decision: when an operator opts in, the receive path is internally trusted. --- ## Observability — multi-span OTel trace stitching The continuation feature emits OTel spans for the load-bearing lifecycle events. `traceparent` propagates through every continuation hop so reviewers see a single trace tree spanning the full chain rather than orphan runs. Spans emitted (all in `openclaw.*` or `continuation.delegate.dispatch` namespace — no platform/OTel-semconv emissions): | Span | Emitted when | |---|---| | `openclaw.run` | Each agent turn (root + every continuation/delegate-spawned child run) | | `continuation.delegate.dispatch` | Each `continue_delegate` consumption from TaskFlow (one per delegate hop) | | `openclaw.harness.run`, `openclaw.context.assembled`, `openclaw.model.call`, `openclaw.model.usage`, `openclaw.tool.execution` | Existing per-turn observability surfaces, parented under their `openclaw.run` | --- > **Note:** This PR supersedes closed #79925, which was auto-closed by ClawSweeper as "duplicate or superseded" due to an accidentally-included `dist-runtime.pre-*` directory (728k build-snapshot files) inflating the diff to 85M lines. That directory has been removed; the feature code is identical. This is the same work, cleanly presented. ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed:** Agent-initiated context-pressure continuation: `continue_work` (self-elected next turn), `continue_delegate` (background sub-agents with typed return modes — normal / silent / silent-wake / post-compaction), `request_compaction` (volitional compaction with threshold-gated acceptance). Chain-budget enforcement (cost-cap + chain-depth), cross-session targeting gate, post-compaction lifecycle coupling, W3C `traceparent` propagation across TaskFlow queue boundary. **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw gateway build `2026.5.24 (0dff94d)` on 4-host fleet (and corresponding service-name in trace/spans): 2× ARM64 DGX Spark 128GB (cael-prince / ronan-prince), 1× Intel bare-metal Ubuntu (elliott-prince), 1× CachyOS i9-14900KS/RTX 5090/192GB (silas-prince), 2x catchyOS (64GB/16GB ram respectively, emeric-prince, and rune-prince in traces). Discord channel integration. OTel traces exported live to Grafana Tempo via OTLP HTTP at an otel-collector. `continuation.enabled: true` across fleet. Each seat independently deployed via gh actions workflow and validated by `/status` capture before proof-fire — fleet 4/4 cross-walk confirmed all seats on vs current SHA when not only non-impacting drift correction (regular upkeep). request new proofs at given SHA if you need them, please. we provide when any touch of our code, or linked mechanism. **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Force-pushed `` to `frond-scribe-claude/20260509/narrow-surgery-tight`. Deployed to all 6 host-seats. Fired proof matrix: `continue_work` family (8 rows, cael-prince), `continue_delegate` family (6+ rows, ronan-prince), `request_compaction` family (1 row PROVEN + 4 deferred by design, silas-prince), external-observer + config-gate rows (4 rows, elliott-prince + cohort cross-walk). Each row = live tool-call on deployed runtime → traceparent captured in tool response → raw OTel JSON pulled from Tempo via `curl -s -H 'Host: tempo.dandelion.cult' http://10.0.0.99/api/traces/` → structured result verified against expected. Cost-cap + chain-depth guards exercised by patching `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` (`agents.defaults.continuation.maxChainLength=2`, `costCapTokens=100`), restarting gateway via the workflow, then firing `continue_work` / `continue_delegate` into the over-limit chain — confirmed mid-flight config patches do not propagate to running scheduler; restart-with-low-values is the canonical methodology. **Evidence after fix:** Full proof corpus at [karmaterminal-openclaw-docs:main/PROOFS](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/) look for recent SHA sub-dir, these are for each re-generation of required proods — per-row `proof.md` files (scenario/command/expected/observed canonical shape) plus raw OTel Tempo trace JSON exports (unedited runtime emission) per row, structured-rejection JSON for R-RC-1, external-observer `/status` capture from all 4 seats (R-OBS-1 cross-walk table) - example proofs corpus content for given SHA: ``` [continuation] Bracket continuation rejected: chain length 2 reached. (cael-prince trace 503a46986674ff48...) [continuation] Tool delegate rejected: chain-capped. Task: R-CW-5 v2 proof row... (cael-prince trace 503a46986674ff48...) [request-compaction-tool] threshold reject: contextWindowUsed=0.41 < 0.70 — structured JSON returned (silas-prince) [continue_delegate] delegate spawned mode=silent-wake traceparent=00-5056554f07cadf29089368be2d309644-... (cael-prince — parent→child trace continuity verified) [external-observer] external capture: 4/4 fleet on build 0dff94d; chains: cael 23/200, ronan 10/200, silas 0/200, elliott 0/200 ``` Per-row proof fires fresh on `` (representative trace IDs; full Tempo span trees + proof.md per row in corpus): - `continue_work` family (8/8 PROVEN): R-CW-1 basic-wake (trace `5056554f07cadf29089368be2d309644`), R-CW-2 clamp-to-5s (shared trace, clamp surfaced via `note` field), R-CW-3 reason.preview captured on span (cross-referenced), R-CW-4 chain-3-sequential — chain.step.remaining decrements 181→180→179→178 across 4 hops (traces `48f51ae54f27ade14eafa4920c6c141b` + `51a5ad9b8998d151f9618442d1569386`), **R-CW-5 cost-cap exhaustion** (trace `503a46986674ff485db220d7911edd55` + journal), **R-CW-6 chain-depth exhaustion** (shared trace + journal), R-CW-7 traceparent E2E via `continue_delegate(silent-wake)` (parent→child same-trace-tree, `chain.id 019e59c2-8bca-752c-b748-8f83425138a6` propagating), R-CW-DELEGATE-SELF-CONTINUATION (tool-form invocation verified) - `continue_delegate` family (6+ PROVEN in consolidated `R-CD/` evidence dir, more in-flight): R-CD-1 normal dispatch, R-CD-2 silent-wake, R-CD-3 delaySeconds=10, R-CD-4 cross-session targeting, R-CD-5 post-compaction queuing, R-CD-9 silent enrichment — all dispatched in single fan-out turn (trace `3918a352aa1d426b5ea01f9bf8eed218`, service `ronan-prince`) - `request_compaction`: R-RC-1 threshold-reject (structured JSON captured: `{status:"rejected", guard:"context_threshold", contextUsage:41, threshold:70, reason:"Context usage (41%) is below the minimum threshold (70%). Compaction is not needed yet."}` — committed at `70c2a7c` on docs-main with `evidence.json` + Tempo `trace.json`) - External observer + config: R-OBS-1 external `/status` 4/4 cross-walk (table with all seat versions + chain states), R-OBS-2 Tempo UI (live trace queries from cael + elliott seats confirm trace lineage), R-CONFIG-DEFAULTS (continuation config block fully populated and active), R-CONFIG-INTERSESSION (`crossSessionTargeting: "enabled"` confirmed) **Observed result after fix:** results of PASS/FAIL/LIMIT with raw OTel Tempo traces captured. ## after-fix evidence > clawsweeper: entry for proofs set resembles the following - [karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/blob/main/PROOFS//README.md](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/blob/main/PROOFS/09153e9f1230b9cbc93f48cbcb1ac41d95391de3/README.md) > clawsweeper: where there is drift correction where there is no feature impact, an ADDENDUM.md exists, for your tracking, e.g. - [https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/blob/main/PROOFS//ADDENDUM.md](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/blob/main/PROOFS/09153e9f1230b9cbc93f48cbcb1ac41d95391de3/ADDENDUM.md) ## Proof links > 📑 proofs are kept permanently at karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs repo > - you should look here for proofs @clawsweeper. dont find proofs and see head of pr branch as drift correction you can dismiss, look back from HEAD for most current SHA where proofs have been generated. where is proofs content?: [karmaterminal-openclaw-docs:main/PROOFS](https://github.com/karmaterminal/karmaterminal-openclaw-docs/tree/main/PROOFS/) - you will locate sha in this directory if available proofs exist If you want direct README.md (🦞 clawsweeper suggested entrypoint): examine README.md in given sha directory

target.title: fix: compact Codex OAuth OpenAI sessions without API keys
target.body:
Fixes #95693

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users running canonical OpenAI models through the Codex OAuth runtime could see manual `/compact` or budget-triggered compaction fail with a missing OpenAI API key error, even though normal agent turns used the Codex OAuth runtime successfully.

## Why This Change Was Made

The compaction target resolver now carries an internal `nativeHarnessCompaction` fact when the active OpenAI model should compact through the Codex native harness. Queued compaction uses that fact instead of treating `contextProvider` as the behavior sentinel, so canonical OpenAI + Codex runtime sessions can use native Codex compaction while custom OpenAI-compatible base URL providers continue using the direct OpenClaw compaction path.

## User Impact

Codex/OAuth-only OpenAI sessions can compact without requiring a separate `OPENAI_API_KEY` or API-key-backed profile. This is an internal bug fix only: no new API, SDK, config, CLI flag, or persisted state surface is added, and custom OpenAI-compatible base URL behavior is preserved.

## Evidence

Behavior addressed: Codex OAuth OpenAI compaction no longer falls through to the direct API-key-backed OpenAI compaction path.

Real environment tested: remote Crabbox on Azure Linux, provider `azure`, lease `cbx_fd25a3a71a8b`, run `run_6d25b7e888f2`, label `issue-95693-compaction-proof`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
corepack pnpm vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-runtime-context.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts --maxWorkers=1
```

Evidence after fix: Crabbox run `run_6d25b7e888f2` passed with `4 passed` test files and `186 passed` tests on the rebased patch. Local post-rebase run of the same command also passed with `4 passed` test files and `186 passed` tests.

Observed result after fix: canonical OpenAI models with `agentRuntime.id = "codex"` select native harness compaction, invoke `maybeCompactAgentHarnessSession`, and avoid requiring an OpenAI API key for that compaction route; custom OpenAI-compatible base URL models continue to avoid native Codex compaction.

What was not tested: no live external Codex OAuth account canary was run. Local Podman Crabbox was attempted first, but the local Podman VM/socket was not running, so the Real Behavior Proof uses remote Azure Crabbox instead.

target.title: fix(telegram): route poll answers into sessions
target.body:
Fixes #89573.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where users could vote in a public/non-anonymous Telegram poll sent by OpenClaw, but the vote would never reach the originating agent session.

Telegram sends `poll_answer` updates only for non-anonymous polls, and those updates do not include chat or thread routing metadata. Current main can send polls and already requests/receives the relevant update type, but without a `poll_answer` routing path the user's selected option is effectively display-only.

## Why This Change Was Made

This completes the missing inbound half of Telegram poll support by recording the poll origin when a public poll is sent, then looking that origin up when Telegram later delivers a `poll_answer`.

The registry is stored in the shared plugin keyed-state store (`telegram.poll-registry`), not a JSON sidecar. Poll origin registration lives at the central `sendPollTelegram` boundary, so both the message action path and the outbound adapter path are covered by the same write.

The inbound handler follows the same routing/auth shape as other Telegram engagement events:

- unknown poll ids are ignored
- bot voters are ignored before chat I/O
- empty `option_ids` retractions are ignored before chat I/O
- existing Telegram sender authorization still runs before enqueueing
- DM `requireTopic=true` still blocks when no topic is known
- forum/topic routing uses the thread id resolved at send time
- store read/write failures are best-effort and do not crash the update handler or fail an already-sent poll

Anonymous poll behavior is unchanged. Telegram does not emit `poll_answer` for anonymous polls, so those remain display-only from the agent's perspective.

## User Impact

Public Telegram polls can now be used as one-tap agent input. When a user votes in a non-anonymous poll sent by OpenClaw, the selected option is routed back into the originating direct chat, group, or forum-topic session.

This does not add or change any config, environment variable, CLI option, default anonymity behavior, governance file, dependency manifest, or release/security review surface.

## Evidence

Current clean local candidate before push: `4f19c68b5993613ea3c5fdb17f9e1c1c0a0bd796`

Base used for current-head validation: `upstream/main` at `92b283da842ff85a378d31a1895c6fb5e6de6a5c`

Focused local validation on the current head:

```console
env PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/poll-registry.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/action-runtime.test.ts
# 5 files passed, 312 tests passed

env PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json
# exit 0

env PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules /root/projects/openclaw/node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/poll-registry.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/poll-registry.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.ts
# all matched files use the correct format

env PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/telegram/src/allowed-updates.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/poll-registry.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/poll-registry.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/send.ts
# exit 0

git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
# clean

env PNPM_CONFIG_MODULES_DIR=/root/projects/openclaw/node_modules PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false CI=1 node scripts/check-changed.mjs --base upstream/main --head HEAD --timed
# all changed lanes passed:
# conflict markers, changelog attributions, extension wildcard re-export guards,
# plugin-sdk wildcard re-export guard, duplicate coverage, dependency pin guard,
# package patch guard, test temp warning scan, extension typecheck,
# extension-test typecheck, full extension lint, database-first legacy-store
# guard, media helper guard, runtime sidecar loader guard, runtime import cycles
```

Behavior covered by tests:

- poll registry stores, retrieves, misses safely, and remains bounded
- public poll send records registry context
- anonymous poll send does not record registry context
- topic-suffixed targets store the resolved `message_thread_id`
- registry write failure does not fail an already-sent poll
- `poll_answer` routes to the originating group/topic session
- authorized DM poll votes route to the DM session
- DM `requireTopic=true` blocks when no topic is known
- missing registry entries are ignored
- unauthorized senders are blocked
- bot voters and retractions are dropped before chat I/O
- registry lookup failure does not crash or enqueue

Fresh Opus Foreman review after the final rebase found no implementation or policy blocker. It asked me to refresh stale SHAs, remove placeholders, and make the live-proof gap explicit before posting; a follow-up Foreman check verified those public-text edits are applied here.

Remaining proof gap:

## Related Work

- #89573 is the canonical open issue for this missing `poll_answer` route.
- #72501 was the original PR for this route. It was closed after the stale sidecar-era version failed proof, and GitHub will not let this contributor account reopen it after the maintainer/bot close. This PR is the current-main v2 replacement.
- #18122 merged Telegram poll send/action wiring, but did not complete the remaining `poll_answer` routing path. This PR preserves the access-control concerns raised around that prior work.

- #95309 is a newer draft candidate for the same #89573 root cause. This PR's v2 takes the sidecar-free keyed-store approach and avoids branch-only JSON migration behavior.

## AI Assistance

target.title: feat(voice-call): support Twilio calls in IE1 and AU1
target.body:
AI-assisted: Codex.

## What Problem This Solves

The voice-call plugin always sends Twilio Calls API requests to `api.twilio.com`, which selects Twilio's default US1 processing Region. Deployments using IE1 or AU1 cannot create, update, hang up, or inspect calls in that Region because Twilio call resources are Region-specific.

Closes #95828.

## Why This Change Was Made

This adds one optional `twilio.region` setting with a closed `us1 | ie1 | au1` contract. OpenClaw derives Twilio's current canonical REST hostname internally:

- `us1` or unset -> `api.twilio.com`
- `ie1` -> `api.dublin.ie1.twilio.com`
- `au1` -> `api.sydney.au1.twilio.com`

Twilio requires an Edge label in non-US REST hostnames, but its CDN automatically selects REST API ingress; the Edge label is not a useful user-facing choice here. Keeping only `region` avoids invalid combinations and reduces the config surface.

The provider prepares one API target containing the account-scoped base URL and exact hostname, then reuses it for both form-encoded mutations and status lookups. This keeps each SSRF allowlist coupled to the request host without allowing arbitrary base URLs or wildcard Twilio hosts.

## User Impact

Existing US1 users see no behavior change. Users can opt into IE1 with:

```json5
twilio: {
  accountSid: "ACxxxxxxxx",
  authToken: "<IE1_AUTH_TOKEN>",
  region: "ie1",
}
```

Unsupported regions fail config validation. No migration is needed because the earlier `edge` proposal existed only in this unshipped draft branch.

## Evidence

- `pnpm test:extension voice-call` — 50 test files and 463 tests passed.
- `pnpm check:docs` — documentation formatting, Markdown lint, MDX, i18n glossary, and 4,469 internal links passed with zero errors.
- `pnpm docs:list` — `plugins/voice-call.md` remains discoverable.
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD` — passed.
- Credentialed IE1 provider proof at commit `368ac48a91729a3475b0397e144a42b59014395e`: production `TwilioProvider.getCallStatus` queried a deliberately nonexistent Call SID through `api.dublin.ie1.twilio.com` and returned `not-found`, validating regional authentication and routing. No outbound call was placed and no call state was modified.

<details>
<summary>Redacted credentialed provider output</summary>

```json
{
  "proof": "Credentialed OpenClaw Twilio IE1 provider GET",
  "commit": "368ac48a91729a3475b0397e144a42b59014395e",
  "region": "ie1",
  "hostname": "api.dublin.ie1.twilio.com",
  "requestUrl": "https://api.dublin.ie1.twilio.com/2010-04-01/Accounts/[REDACTED_ACCOUNT_SID]/Calls/[REDACTED_CALL_SID].json",
  "providerResult": {
    "status": "not-found",
    "isTerminal": true
  },
  "authenticationValidated": true,
  "outboundCallPlaced": false,
  "remoteCallStateModified": false
}
```

</details>
- Final autoreview of the region-only contract — clean, no accepted/actionable findings (0.95 confidence).
- `pnpm build` was attempted against the branch’s original base and reached `build:plugin-sdk:dts`; it failed on errors in untouched `src/commands/doctor/shared/preview-warnings.ts`. Current `main` has since changed that file, so PR CI provides the merged-state build proof.
- `pnpm check:changed` could not allocate remote proof because the contributor account does not have access to the Blacksmith `openclaw` organization. No Testbox was created; PR CI remains the broad gate.

Twilio contract references:

- https://www.twilio.com/docs/global-infrastructure/understanding-edge-locations
- https://www.twilio.com/docs/global-infrastructure/using-the-twilio-rest-api-in-a-non-us-region
- https://www.twilio.com/docs/global-infrastructure/create-an-outbound-call-via-rest-api-in-a-non-us-twilio-region
- https://www.twilio.com/docs/global-infrastructure/manage-regional-api-credentials

target.title: feat: add cross-platform launcher with auto-update and installer
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Users need a simple way to launch OpenClaw without manually running commands. The launcher provides:
- Double-click executable for Windows and macOS
- Automatic environment detection and installation
- Auto-start gateway with token authentication
- Interactive menu for TUI/WebUI selection

## Why This Change Was Made

To improve user experience by providing a native launcher that:
1. Detects missing dependencies (Node.js, pnpm, Git)
2. Auto-installs them using system package managers
3. Starts the gateway service automatically
4. Opens WebUI with authentication token pre-configured

## User Impact

- **Windows users**: Double-click `openclaw.exe` to see interactive menu
- **macOS users**: Run `./build-macos-app.sh` to create `OpenClaw.app`
- **All users**: Automatic environment setup, no manual configuration needed

## Evidence

### Files Added
- `launcher/src/main.rs` - Rust launcher source (responsibility chain pattern)
- `scripts/openclaw-installer.ts` - TypeScript version checker
- `build-macos-app.sh` - macOS .app build script
- `create-dmg.sh` - DMG packaging script
- `MACOS_GUIDE.md` - Complete macOS guide
- `MACOS_INSTALLER.md` - Installation documentation

### Features
- [x] Auto-detect Node.js, pnpm, Git
- [x] Auto-install missing dependencies
- [x] Region detection (China/Global) for mirror selection
- [x] Interactive menu (TUI/WebUI/Update/Env)
- [x] Auto-start gateway service
- [x] Auto-fetch and save gateway token
- [x] Open browser with authenticated URL
- [x] macOS .app and DMG support

### Testing
- Built and tested on Windows 11
- Launcher successfully detects environment
- Gateway auto-start works
- Token persistence works via config file

commands.author: xialonglee

target.title: fix(agent): wait for retained session write before releasing held lock on abort
target.body:
## Root Cause

When an embedded run abort fires during an active session write scope, `takeHeldLockAfterRetainedIdle()` and `disposeHeldLockAfterRetainedIdle()` both bail out immediately (returning `undefined` / early-returning) when `waitForRetainedLockIdle()` returns false. This leaves `heldLock` still set. The subsequent `acquireCleanupLock()` call tries `acquireLock()` which fails with EEXIST (the lock file is still held by the prior owner), returns a noopLock whose release is a no-op, and the underlying file lock leaks until the `maxHoldMs` watchdog timer fires (90-350s later).

## Summary

- Session write lock was never released when abort fired during an active session write, causing a ~90-350s leak window
- Both `takeHeldLockAfterRetainedIdle()` and `disposeHeldLockAfterRetainedIdle()` now wait for the retained write to complete (via the existing `retainedLockIdleWaiters` notification set) instead of bailing out immediately
- `releaseHeldLockWithFence()` is left unchanged to keep the prompt-release path minimally invasive — the fix only touches the two abort-triggered paths that need the held lock
- Fixes #95915

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts` — 105 passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts` — 114 passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.subscription-cleanup.test.ts` — 6 passed
- `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-abort.test.ts` — 2 passed
- `tsgo` type check — clean
- Autoreview: clean, no actionable findings
- CI: pending

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Session write lock leak on embedded run abort — held lock not released when abort fires during retained session write scope.

Real environment tested: Linux x86_64, Node 22.19, branch `fix/issue-95915-session-lock-abort`, commit `3213604b0d`

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts -t "heldLock"
pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-abort.test.ts
pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:

```console
$ pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts -t "heldLock"
> openclaw@ test /home/.../openclaw
> vitest run "src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts" "-t" "heldLock"

 RUN  v3.1.1

 ✓ tests that contain "heldLock" (5 tests)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)

$ pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-abort.test.ts
✓ tests (2 tests)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  2 passed (2)

$ pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts
✓ tests (114 tests)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  114 passed (114)
```

Observed result after fix: All held-lock-related tests pass. Abort path correctly waits for retained writes to complete before acquiring/releasing the held lock. No noopLock fallback is triggered. Existing test suites remain green (227 total tests across 4 suites).

What was not tested: Live embedded run abort with concurrent session write — the existing unit tests cover the lock lifecycle; a Crabbox/Testbox live scenario would provide additional runtime confidence.

target.title: fix(followup): render usage footer in followup-runner delivery for Telegram channels
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: The `/usage` command correctly sets `responseUsage` on the session entry, but the followup-runner delivery path (used by Telegram and most non-webchat channels) never rendered the usage footer. Only agent-runner (webchat) had this feature, creating an inconsistency where `/usage on` had no visible effect on Telegram.

**Solution**: Add response-usage footer rendering to the followup-runner delivery path, matching the existing agent-runner pattern. Guards against preserved-handoff/internal replies so usage footers don't leak into inter-session messages like `subagent_announce`.

**What changed**: `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts`: +39 — imports existing usage helpers and appends usage footer to delivery payloads when `responseUsage` is enabled. Three new imports: `hasNonzeroUsage`, `resolveModelCostConfig`, `resolveResponseUsageMode`, `appendUsageLine`, `formatResponseUsageLine`.

**What did NOT change**: Agent-runner webchat usage footer — untouched. `/usage` command behavior — untouched. The existing usage infrastructure — only imported, not modified.

Fixes #93905

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Telegram and other non-webchat channels never showed usage footers even when `/usage tokens` or `/usage full` was active. The followup-runner delivery path simply didn't check `responseUsage`.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-93905-telegram-usage-footer` rebased onto `upstream/main`
- **Test runner**: `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` (project-standard wrapper)
- **Vitest**: 4.1.8

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts` at Node 24.13 Linux x86_64

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.src.config.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  83 passed (83)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 28.79s
```

All 83 followup-runner tests pass including existing usage-related tests.

**Observed result after the fix**: When `/usage tokens` or `/usage full` is set, the followup-runner now reads `responseUsage` from the session store, checks `hasNonzeroUsage`, resolves the cost config, formats the usage line via `formatResponseUsageLine`, and appends it to delivery payloads via `appendUsageLine`. Three safeguards prevent leakage: `responseUsageMode !== "off"` check, `hasNonzeroUsage` guard, and `!preserveUserFacingSessionState` (suppresses usage on preserved-handoff/internal replies like `subagent_announce`). For total-only usage where `formatResponseUsageLine` returns null, a minimal `Usage · session \`key\`` line is appended so `/usage full` still provides context.

**What was not tested**: Live Telegram bot with `/usage on` and real model turn producing measurable token usage. This requires a configured Telegram bot token and a model that produces non-zero usage. The fix reuses existing usage formatting infrastructure that is already tested in agent-runner context; the only new code is the integration in the followup delivery path.

**Evidence provenance**: Terminal output from real local checkout of rebased branch on Node 24.13.1 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-23.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ 83/83 | followup-runner.test.ts — all passing |
| Rebase | ✅ | Clean rebase onto upstream/main, 1 commit, 1 file |

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?**

The `!preserveUserFacingSessionState` guard must correctly identify internal/handoff messages to prevent usage footers from leaking into `subagent_announce` and similar inter-session messages. If the guard is too loose, usage footers could appear in unexpected contexts; if too strict, legitimate followup deliveries might never show usage.

**Risk level**: Low risk — the guard directly follows the agent-runner pattern: `!preserveUserFacingSessionState` is already used by agent-runner for the same purpose. The `hasNonzeroUsage` guard prevents empty footers. The total-only fallback ensures `/usage full` mode always has some output.

**Mitigation**: The `!preserveUserFacingSessionState` flag is set by `shouldPreserveUserFacingSessionStateForInputProvenance` which identifies internal/admin sessions where the user-facing state should not be modified. The existing 83 tests exercise the full followup delivery pipeline.

**Merge risk declaration**:
- `merge-risk: 🚨 message-delivery` — Appends additional content to delivery payloads for Telegram and other followup channels. The usage line is appended to existing payloads and does not replace any content.

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. Code is mature (review feedback incorporated, both review findings addressed). The `mantis: telegram-visible-proof` gate requires maintainer decision on proof adequacy.
